metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
1
Introduction
This chapter serves as an introduction to the work presented in this report. Section 1.1 is intended to
provide the reader with the context in which this study is framed. Section 1.2, highlights the importance
of the main problem on which this study is focused. Finally, Section 1.3 presents the goals pursued by
this work (research questions) and introduces the structure of this report.
1.1. Historical background
It is widely recognised that the human space exploration era strongly stimulated scientific research and
lead to important breakthroughs in very diverse fields of study. The occurrence that made possible
such scientific revolution was the possibility of observing, for the first time in history of humankind,
the planet as a whole from the point of view of artificial satellites orbiting the Earth. It provided the
scientific community with precious data that would allow the analysis of the natural processes that
take place in the planet on a completely new global scale. At a later stage, it opened the way to the
exploration of the Solar System and the universe. [ 1]
Two important challenges, which are strongly related and have been present in space missions

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
exploration of the Solar System and the universe. [ 1]
Two important challenges, which are strongly related and have been present in space missions
from the very beginning of the history of space exploration, are tracking and orbit determination of the
satellites. On the one hand, satellite tracking refers to the measurement of a satellite’s position and
velocity with respect to a convenient reference frame. On the other hand, orbit determination refers
to the calculation of the orbit of a body in space based on certain input observations such as those
provided by tracking techniques. These two challenges were, and still are nowadays, crucial for the
execution of every space mission as well as for the achievement of its scientific goals. Many techniques
were developed in those years to satisfy such critical needs.
Nevertheless, the problem of orbit determination had been faced many years in advance with the
focus on understanding the motion of natural celestial bodies as observed from the Earth. As an exam-
ple, the work of the mathematician Carl Friedrich Gauss at that times was of remarkable importance,

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
ple, the work of the mathematician Carl Friedrich Gauss at that times was of remarkable importance,
developing innovative techniques based on which the astronomers F . Von Zach and H. W . Olbers, in-
dependently from each other , were able to successfully compute the orbit of Ceres (on 31 December
1801 and on 2 January 1802, respectively). [ 2]
Notwithstanding the excellence of those achievements, the techniques applied in those dates would
most likely have never been able to track low mass, small size bodies such as man-made satellites.
In this sense, the space era changed the rules of the game by making possible the installation of
specialised artificial equipment aboard the satellites providing unprecedented levels of accuracy in the
tracking data as well as the acquisition of invaluable data for scientific purposes.
Nowadays, a wide variety of satellite tracking techniques are available, each of them with different
advantages, drawbacks and complexities. Sometimes, different techniques are applied depending on
the phase of the mission. It is also possible to combine different tracking data types for navigation.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
the phase of the mission. It is also possible to combine different tracking data types for navigation.
Range and Doppler tracking are, by far , the most common tracking methods, being present in the vast
majority of space missions.
In the coming years, it is expected that the requirements for spacecraft navigation systems in inter-
planetary missions will become more and more demanding, aiming at higher performance, reliability,
1

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
2 1. Introduction
robustness and cost-effectiveness levels. This imposes a new challenge for the development of new
(or the improvement of already existent) tracking technologies that, by satisfying such severe demand,
contribute to the achievent of the scientific goals of the future. [ 3]
1.2. Importance of tracking in space missions
In order to successfully perform the manoeuvres (if any) that modify the trajectory of an artificial body
in space according to its mission plan, it is needed to precisely know its position and velocity relative to
the Sun, the Earth or other convenient reference frame. This means that navigation is a prerequisite
for guidance and makes evident the importance of accurate tracking in space missions. [ 4]
The same applies to the execution of scientific observations on-board. Precise tracking of spacecraft
is not only crucial for their operation, but it is also crucial for accurate processing of the scientific data
collected by the instruments with which they are outfitted. Any error in the state vector of the spacecraft
can potentially propagate into the derived scientific products obtained (among other inputs) from the
analysis of tracking data.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
can potentially propagate into the derived scientific products obtained (among other inputs) from the
analysis of tracking data.
The level of error in the tracking observations of a spacecraft also affects the adjustment of the
parameters of the dynamical and observation models in the least squares process that pursues the
best fit between the actual observations and the models. Those parameters can be used to infer
features of the operating environment of the spacecraft, evidencing the importance of accurate tracking
for accomplishing scientific objectives in space missions. An detailed explanation of the spacecraft
navigation process can be consulted in Section 2.1 of [ 3].
An example of scientific product derived from accurate tracking of spacecraft is the measurement
of the gravitational field of a body, that can be obtained by analysing the gravitational accelerations
acting on the orbiter . It gives also some insight to investigate the inner structure of the central body.
Other example application is the measurement of atmospheric properties (if the central body has an
atmosphere). Finally, knowing the gravitational forces acting on a spacecraft allows testing gravitation

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
atmosphere). Finally, knowing the gravitational forces acting on a spacecraft allows testing gravitation
theories in the relativistic sense. [ 1]
A brief overview of the most common tracking techniques is given in Section 2.1.
1.3. Research motivation and questions
Generally, satellites as well as deep space probes are outfitted with a telecommunications system that
performs two main functions: the first one, uplink, is to transmit commands from a ground station to
control the spacecraft; the second, downlink, is to return its telemetry signals from space to a/some
ground station(s). This system is used for two clearly dissimilar purposes: radio science and commu-
nication with the spacecraft.
Radio science refers to the analysis of the telemetry signals for diverse investigations such as de-
termination of mass properties of natural celestial bodies, measurement of planetary atmospheres or
ionospheres, study of planetary shapes and more. On some occasions it also refers to the execution
of relativity tests.
On the other hand, the radio telecommunication system is used to obtain the range, velocity and

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of relativity tests.
On the other hand, the radio telecommunication system is used to obtain the range, velocity and
angular information of the spacecraft to support its navigation. For this end, the tracking systems usu-
ally provide a variety of radiometric data like Doppler , range and/or interferometry data, depending the
type of tracking data needed for each application on the purpose application itself. Some applications
tolerate different tracking data types for the same goal. [ 5]
As reported by Iess et al. in [ 6], in the last decades various tracking data types have been used, one
of the most widely used being Doppler observations. The Doppler effect due to the relative motion of the
radio elements can be retrieved from the transmitted signal by applying different technologies, yielding
different observables, in particular: open- and closed-loop Doppler data. The former is obtained from
Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI - see explanation in Section 2.1) observations while the latter
is typically obtained by the ground-based antennas of NASA’s Deep Space Network (DSN).
The relation between both data types is often confused during data processing. In addition, many

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
The relation between both data types is often confused during data processing. In addition, many
software packages are only capable of processing the more widely used closed-loop Doppler data.
Since both data types are closely related, it is possible to process open-loop data making use tools
designed for closed-loop data by applying convenient corrections. In this study, the results obtained
from experimenting with two such approaches are presented. [ 7]

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
1.3. Research motivation and questions 3
The work presented in this report is based on computer simulations of Earth-based planetary space-
craft tracking scenarios. I opted to develop my own scripts, as discussed in Section 4.1, and I also
made use of Tudat 1 for part of the simulations.
The top-level science question of this research is: is it possible to convert open-loop Doppler data
into equivalent closed-loop Doppler observations by means of a mathematical model? . However , the
goals of this study are multiple, addressing the following questions:
• How can equivalent closed-loop Doppler data be obtained, by means of a mathematical model,
from an open-loop Doppler data set?
• What is/are the conversion performance(s) of such mathematical model(s)?
• How do the parameters of the model(s) influence its/their conversion performance(s)?
• Do the characteristics of the input data set influence the performance of the conversion model(s)?
If so, how?
• Is there any systematic approach to optimise the performance(s) of the conversion model(s)?
The core of this report is comprised of Chapters 3 to 5. Chapter 3 introduces the data-type conver-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
The core of this report is comprised of Chapters 3 to 5. Chapter 3 introduces the data-type conver-
sion algorithms that have been implemented and tested, as well as the strategy followed to assess their
performances. Chapter 4 details how each of the two algorithms has been implemented in Matlab, and
it includes an explanation of how the models and their implementations have been validated as well.
Chapter 5 presents the results obtained for the Doppler data types conversion performed with input
Doppler data generated by simulating a huge set of different scenarios and includes a discussion of
the reasons behind the observed behaviour or trends of the performances of each of the two studied
data-type conversion approaches.
In Chapter 2, a description of the most common existing spacecraft tracking techniques for planetary
missions is given, as well as a discussion of the different ways to retrieve the Doppler effect information
from the transmitted signal between two radio elements that are in relative motion, providing theoretical
background for Chapters 3 to 5.
Finally, Chapter 6 summarises the conclusions drawn from this study and includes some recommen-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
background for Chapters 3 to 5.
Finally, Chapter 6 summarises the conclusions drawn from this study and includes some recommen-
dations on how this work could be further developed, improved or complemented in the future.
1Tudat stands for TU Delft Astrodynamics T oolbox . It comprises a set of C ዄዄ software libraries developed and maintained by
members of the Astrodynamics & Space Missions research group at the Faculty of Aerospace Engineering of Delft University of
T echnology (The Netherlands), aimed at providing its users with functionality to simulate astrodynamics applications.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
2
Doppler data
Tracking of bodies (either artificial or natural) in space, i.e knowing their position and velocity with
respect to a convenient (any of interest) reference frame, can be done by several different techniques
(note that, even though observations are taken with respect to the observer , they might be referred to
any reference frame of interest provided that the geometric/cinematic relation between the observer
and the new reference frame is known). In section 2.1, a brief introduction to the working principles
of the most common spacecraft tracking techniques is provided, with the focus on radiometric tracking
techniques. Section 2.2 introduces two different types of Doppler data that can be obtained depend-
ing on the technology applied to extract the Doppler shift information from the radiometric signals
transmitted between radio elements that are in relative motion.
2.1. Spacecraft tracking techniques
A first classification of spacecraft tracking techniques might be into optical or radiometric.
Optical tracking refers to all the optical techniques that allow the tracking of satellites. Doing it from

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
Optical tracking refers to all the optical techniques that allow the tracking of satellites. Doing it from
the Earth has two significant limitations: the resolution of the instruments, which is inherent to the
observation methodology; and the opportunities to execute such observations, which depend on the
relative position of the Earth, the Sun and the satellite so that light rays reflected by the satellite stand
out of its background and can be observed from the Earth, as stated in [ 8]. In the beginning of the space
exploration age, naked-eye observations were used as backup for tracking the first Sputniks, as reported
in [ 9]. A different optical approach consisted of the use of theodolites, devices that allow measuring
the angular coordinates of the observed body. Nowadays, the most widely used optical method for
spacecraft tracking relies on on-board cameras that take images of the target body which after proper
computer processing are the preferred tracking data for the approach and landing navigation phases of
space missions. This type of observations are critical for missions to small size target bodies, according

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
space missions. This type of observations are critical for missions to small size target bodies, according
to [ 10]. Optical tracking also includes laser ranging techniques, that allow measuring ranges based
on precise measurements of the light travel times of transmitted laser pulses that are reflected by the
tracked body and are received back at the tracking station.
On the other hand, radiometric techniques rely on the transmission of electromagnetic signals
between radio elements (usually one on the Earth and other in the spacecraft at least) to obtain the
precious information of the spacecraft’s state vector . Currently, the signals used most often belong to
the X (≈7-8.5 GHz) and/or Ka (≈35 GHz) bands of the electromagnetic spectrum. These techniques
involve huge, complex infrastructure. The two main antenna networks for communication between the
Earth and space devices are ESA’s Deep Space Tracking Network (ESTRACK) and NASA’s Deep Space
Network (DSN), both of them consisting of a set of worldwide spread large antennas that provide the
communication link essential for the execution of space missions. Detailed information about these

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
communication link essential for the execution of space missions. Detailed information about these
antenna networks can be found in [ 11], [ 12] and Chapter 4 of [ 13].
The two most common observables obtained by means of radiometric techniques are range and
range rate. The basis for obtaining the range observable is the measurement of the light travel time
of the radiometric signal between transmitter and receiver . Knowing the speed of light in vacuum,
a first approximation of the range can be easily obtained. Several corrections are usually applied to
5

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
6 2. Doppler data
account for perturbations caused by the transmission media. The range-rate or Doppler observable is
obtained by measuring the frequency of the received signal at the receiver . As a consequence of the
relative motion between the two radio elements (spacecraft and ground station), the transmitted and
received frequencies differ by what is known as Doppler shift, an example manifestation of the Doppler
effect. By comparing the transmission frequency against that of the received signal, the velocity of
the satellite along the line of sight can be approximated. Depending on the technology applied to
extract the Doppler shift information from the received signal, two different sorts of Doppler data can
be obtained: open- or closed-loop, which are introduced in Section 2.2.
Doppler data, being of differential nature, provides the most valuable information for understanding
the motion of the orbiter around its central body (not informing about how far is the orbiter or its central
body with respect to the observer), while range and angular coordinates observations, being of absolute

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
body with respect to the observer), while range and angular coordinates observations, being of absolute
nature, are the most convenient data types for identification of the position of the central body with
respect to the Earth (thus complementing the information not provided by Doppler data). As reported
by Iess et al. in [ 6], the accuracy of NASA’s and ESA’s tracking systems nowadays is of the order of
1 − 5𝑚 for ranging and 0.1𝑚𝑚/𝑠 at 60s integration time for Doppler .
Several configurations of the telecommunication link can been used for radiometric tracking [ 14].
The so-called one-way tracking mode consists of only a signal path (up-link or down-link) between
the ground station and the spacecraft. The range of the signal is measured and its received Doppler
frequency at the receiver is compared against a locally generated coherent signal allowing the analysis
of the Doppler shift and consequently the computation of the range rate. The observables obtained
by this mode usually include some error due to the clock bias between the ground-based and the on-
board clocks. Another very common tracking mode is two-way (Figure 2.1 a). It comprises two signal

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
board clocks. Another very common tracking mode is two-way (Figure 2.1 a). It comprises two signal
paths (up-link and down-link). In this configuration the ground station sends a signal to the spacecraft,
which sends back to the ground station a coherent signal. The computation of the range and Doppler
observables is analogous to the one-way case. This configuration has two advantages: unlike one-
way radio tracking, this does not require a precise synchronisation of the ground and on-board clocks
since any clock bias can be corrected in the computation of the observables. Additionally, it allows the
correction of the disturbance caused by the propagation media if the uplink and downlink frequencies
are different, because that phenomenon is frequency dependant. However , this configuration has
some drawbacks: it requires the ground stations to work in diplexer mode, which is more complex
and includes extra noise in the signal, instead of just in listening mode. Moreover , unlike the one-way
mode, this would not allow simultaneous tracking of several satellites (using only one ground station)

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
mode, this would not allow simultaneous tracking of several satellites (using only one ground station)
orbiting the same body with the consequent savings in ground resources. In addition, this configuration
is not suitable for tracking satellites very far from the Earth. In such cases, the round-trip light travel
time might be so long that the ground station, due to Earth’s spin, is on the opposite site by the time
the signal comes back [ 3]. A solution to this issue is receiving the return signal at a different ground
station, which, indeed, is known as three-way Doppler (Figure 2.1 b). This tracking mode requires
some extra effort to correct the clock-bias between the two ground stations involved. Finally, some
specific missions may require using an extra satellite as an intermediate step linking the communication
of other satellite with a ground station. This is known as four-way tracking mode (Figure 2.1 c) due to
the four signal paths involved.
Figure 2.1: Schematic representation of two-way (a), three-way (b) and four-way (c) configurations of the telecommunication
link for radiometric tracking. [ 1]

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
2.2. The Doppler observable 7
Additionally, from range and range-rate observations, which are radial data types, angular informa-
tion of the spacecraft can be derived due to the impinged signature of Earth’s rotation on the recorded
signal. However , there are other tracking techniques such as Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI)
that provide much more accurate angular information. This technique is based on the analysis of the
signals emitted by specific sources (the spacecraft and a nearby natural reference source like a quasar)
and received at several ground stations whose relative position is known. By measuring the light time
travel delay of the signals, the angular coordinates of the spacecraft can be obtained by trigonomet-
ric relations (Figure 2.2) provided that the positions of the ground stations and the natural reference
source are known.
Figure 2.2: Scheme for angular positioning of spacecraft applying the VLBI tracking technique. The scheme represents the
simultaneous observation from the Earth of a spacecraft and a natural reference source. The differential signal arrival time ( Ꭱ) is

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
simultaneous observation from the Earth of a spacecraft and a natural reference source. The differential signal arrival time ( Ꭱ) is
obtained by simple differentiation of the light travel times of the signal received at both extremes of the baseline (B). The theta
angle ( ᎕ ) stands for the position angle of the spacecraft relative to the reference source. [ 15]
Different VLBI observable types can be obtained depending on how the cycle ambiguity is solved.
A description of several approaches for this purpose as well as a comparison against each other can
be found in [ 15].
It must be noted that apart from the active radiometric techniques that involve active equipment
on-board the spacecraft to generate radiometric signals (like all the previously introduced radiometric
techniques), there also exist passive techniques (without any active equipment generating radiometric
signals in the target body) that are mainly used for tracking natural celestial bodies like planets and
are crucial for obtaining their ephemeris. However , no more information about passive techniques is
included here since they are not of interest in the frame of this study. An introduction to them can be

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
included here since they are not of interest in the frame of this study. An introduction to them can be
consulted in Section 2.1 of [ 1].
As a final note, it is convenient to point out that, as reported by Thorton and Border in [ 3], the
simultaneous application of different tracking data types for spacecraft navigation leads to higher levels
of reliability and robustness.
2.2. The Doppler observable
The Doppler shift is a manifestation of the Doppler effect that takes place whenever there is relative
motion between two radio elements that are communicating via radiometric signals.
As introduced in the previous section, different sorts of Doppler observable can be obtained de-
pending on the technology applied to extract the Doppler shift information of a signal received at a
ground station from a spacecraft that is in relative motion with respect to it. Two such sorts of Doppler
observable are open- and closed-loop Doppler , whose transformation from one data type into the other
is the focus of this study.
As reported by Bocanegra-Bahamón et al. in [ 16], what is known as closed-loop Doppler data is the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
is the focus of this study.
As reported by Bocanegra-Bahamón et al. in [ 16], what is known as closed-loop Doppler data is the
Doppler observable obtained by measuring the time evolution of the light travel time of the transmitted
radiometric signal based on specialised electronics equipment capable of sensing the total phase change
of the received signal along a specific time interval (count interval) originating a measurement of the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
8 2. Doppler data
total range change along that period. From that output, the frequency evolution of the received signal
from the spacecraft is be obtained.
On the other hand, what is known as open-loop Doppler data is the Doppler observable obtained
from the post-processing of the recording of the frequency spectrum of the received radiometric signal.
The post-processing steps emulate the function of the electronics equipment used for closed-loop
Doppler data wit the advantage that it tolerates abrupt changes in the received signal that would lead
to failure of the closed-loop tracking approach. For the post-processing of the recorded frequency
spectrum data, several different approaches are available.
The main difference between the two introduced Doppler data types is that closed-loop Doppler
data is available on real time as the received radiometric signal is sensed by specialised electronics (it
does not require post-processing), while open-loop Doppler data is obtained a posteriori, after some
post-processing effort. Additionally, open-loop Doppler data is more suitable than closed-loop Doppler

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
post-processing effort. Additionally, open-loop Doppler data is more suitable than closed-loop Doppler
data for certain radio science applications; and closed-loop Doppler data, not requiring post-processing,
is typically used for navigation tracking, as reported by Bocanegra Bahamon et al. in [ 16].
A more detailed explanation of these Doppler observable types can be found in [ 16].
2.2.1. Observation models
This section is aimed at introducing the mathematical equations that model the physical phenomenon
of spacecraft range and Doppler tracking, relating the dynamics of both the ground and the space
segments of the radio link to the tracking observables.
Range and Doppler fundamentals
In order to provide a general explanation with validity considering relativistic effects, it is needed two
distinguish two different time scales. The absolute time coordinate will be designated as t along this
section, while 𝜏 will be the symbol for the proper times measured by an observer . The difference be-
tween the two is that the absolute time scale allows the comparison of proper times, that are dependant
on the observer who measured them.

metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
tween the two is that the absolute time scale allows the comparison of proper times, that are dependant
on the observer who measured them.
A radio communication link composed of a ground station and an orbiting spacecraft is considered.
𝐴 represents a transmitting station, with position vector rA, while 𝐵 represents a receiving spacecraft,
with position rB. Figure 2.3 shows a schematic space-time diagram with the two observers representing
the two ends (ground (A) and space (B) segments) of the radio link. [ 1]
The ground station periodically transmits radiometric signals and records the transmission times.
Those signals are received at the spacecraft (uplink) that records the reception times. The computation
of the difference between the transmission and reception times of the radiometric signals yields the
range observables.
Figure 2.3: Schematic spacetime diagram representing one-way range between reference points A (transmitter) and B (receiver),
both moving at constant velocity. Adapted from [ 1].

the following relations [ 7]:

Relation between Doppler data types
The open-loop Doppler observable ℎ፨, representing the instantaneous frequency change of the received

exactly by equation 2.4. Hence, the following relation can be written:

The closed-loop Doppler data obtained by application of the observation model described by Equa-

text:
3
Doppler data type conversion
As explained in Chapter 1, depending on the technology applied to retrieve the Doppler shift information
from the radiometric signal received at one of two radio elements that are in relative motion, different
sorts of Doppler observable can be obtained. Additionally, as commented in the motivation for this
research (Section 1.3), the relation between open- and closed-loop Doppler data types (introduced in
Section 2) is often confused by the scientific community in data analysis phases, and the software tools
used for processing Doppler data usually are not flexible enough to process both types of Doppler data.
In this chapter , two different Doppler data type conversion processes (proposed by Dirkx et al. in
[7]) to translate from a data type into the other are introduced as well as the strategy followed to
assess the performance of such data type conversions. In Section 3.1 the two different mathematical
approaches/models for the data type conversion are presented. Section 3.2 explains the strategy
followed for the analysis of the conversion performances of the two models. The next chapters describe

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
followed for the analysis of the conversion performances of the two models. The next chapters describe
the software development effort carried out to implement both data type conversion algorithms and
the experimentation that has been done with them to analyse their conversion performances.
3.1. Data type conversion models
Two different mathematical approaches have been implemented and tested for the conversion of open-
loop Doppler observations into equivalent closed-loop Doppler data.
Section 3.1.1 introduces a mathematical approach based on numerical quadrature/integration to
convert an open-loop data set into an equivalent closed-loop data set, whilst Section 3.1.2 introduces
a different approach consisting of the addition of some corrective terms to the closed-loop observation
model in order to obtain the equivalent open-loop Doppler observable.
Finally, in Sections 3.1.3 and 3.1.4, the concepts of numerical differentiation and quadrature, respec-
tively, are briefly discussed for completeness, since both of them play a crucial role in the mathematical
models.
3.1.1. Integration-based model : data conversion.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

3.1.3. Numerical differentiation
In many engineering problems (for example, in optimisation) it is needed to calculate the derivative
of some measured variables. In the real world, it is very common that physical phenomena do not

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of some measured variables. In the real world, it is very common that physical phenomena do not
exactly follow the mathematical equations that model their behaviour due to more or less significant
miss-modelled factors, making it difficult to find analytic expressions for the derivatives of the variables
of interest. In addition, it is very common that the available measurements are sampled at certain
limited discrete times. It is in these kind of situations when numerical differentiation methods become
very useful. [ 19]
The basic idea behind many of these methods is to find an interpolating polynomial that fits the
available data and to approximate the derivative of the available data as the derivative (analytic) of
the considered interpolating polynomial. An advantage of this approach is that it is possible to math-
ematically bound the truncation error due to the applied approximation. Round-off errors are also
present when using floating point arithmetic (as most conventional programs do) for operations with
real numbers. [ 20]
By making use of T aylor series, many different approximations for any order derivative can be de-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
real numbers. [ 20]
By making use of T aylor series, many different approximations for any order derivative can be de-
veloped. As explained by Heinbockel in [ 21], the 𝑗th derivative of a function 𝑦(𝑥) can be approximated
by assuming that it can be expressed as follows:
𝑦(፣)(𝑥።) = 1
ℎ፣ [𝛽፦ 𝑦(𝑥። − 𝑚ℎ) + 𝛽፦ዅኻ 𝑦(𝑥። − (𝑚 − 1)ℎ) + ... + 𝛽ኻ𝑦(𝑥። − ℎ) + 𝛼ኺ𝑦(𝑥።) + ...
+ 𝛼፧ዅኻ 𝑦(𝑥። + (𝑛 − 1)ℎ) + 𝛼፧ 𝑦(𝑥። + 𝑛ℎ)] + 𝑜(ℎፍ ) (3.5)
expression that implicates (𝑚+𝑛+1) equispaced points, where 𝛽፦ , 𝛽፦ዅኻ ,..., 𝛽ኻ, 𝛼ኺ, 𝛼ኻ,..., 𝛼፧ and 𝑁 are
unknowns to be determined (𝑁 is a function of the amount of data points in the stencil and the derivative
order of the derivative that is being approximated), and ℎ is the x-coordinate distance between two
consecutive data points. Denoting 𝑦።ዄ፣ = 𝑦(𝑥። + 𝑗ℎ) with 𝑗 ranging over 𝑗 = −𝑚, −(𝑚 − 1), ..., (𝑛 − 1), 𝑛
and by expanding these terms in T aylor series and substituting them into Eq. 3.5, a new equation is
obtained whose terms can be rearranged to force its right-hand side (of the new version of Eq. 3.5) to
equal the left-hand side of Eq. 3.5 by conveniently setting the coefficients (unknowns) to either zero

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
equal the left-hand side of Eq. 3.5 by conveniently setting the coefficients (unknowns) to either zero
or one. This procedure yields a system of 𝑚 + 𝑛 + 1equations from which the coefficients 𝛽፦ , 𝛽፦ዅኻ ,...,
𝛽ኻ, 𝛼ኺ, 𝛼ኻ,..., 𝛼፧ and the order 𝑁 of the error term can be obtained.
Conveniently determining the amount of terms to be included in the T aylor series expansions is
a problem-dependant choice to be made by the user . The amount of included terms of the T aylor
expansions determines the truncation error level of the derived approximation for the derivative.
There is a huge amount of bibliography about this topic, like for example [ 22] and [ 23], where
many different approximations for every order derivative and with different orders of accuracy can be

One last important note regarding the computation of the numerical derivatives is about the selection
of the parameter ℎ. It determines the total error level of the computed derivatives, by impacting on
both its round-off error and the truncation error of the applied approximation (stencil).
Detailed information about the error analysis of numerical derivatives is easily accessible in spe-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
Detailed information about the error analysis of numerical derivatives is easily accessible in spe-
cialised bibliography, as for example in [ 20]. Regarding the selection of the parameter ℎ, for this
study, it has been optimised experimentally for every involved order derivative as explained in Section
3.2.2.
3.1.4. Numerical integration or quadrature
In numerical analysis, numerical integration is also known as quadrature due to the old practice of
doing squares with the same area as certain plain surfaces, as reported by Heinbockel in [ 21].
Analogously to the explanation for numerical differentiation methods, it is very common in engineer-
ing problems the need of the integral of a measured quantity that in many cases is sampled at certain
discrete times. In addition, there exist functions that can not be integrated analytically, and there
could be also cases where, even existing an analytic expression for the integral of a function, it might
be more computationally efficient to approximate it by applying numerical methods than calculating
its exact value. For these reasons, and due to the fact that it is possible to numerically approximate

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
its exact value. For these reasons, and due to the fact that it is possible to numerically approximate
almost every definite integral, numerical integration methods are widely used.
The numerical calculation of a definite integral of a function 𝑓(𝑥) consists of the approximation
of the area enclosed between the curve representing the function and the x-coordinate axis. This
approximation can be done, conceptually, in many different ways. Figure 3.1 shows several such ways.
The basic idea for the derivation of many numerical integration methods is analogous to the one
explained for the numerical differentiation methods. Based on a discrete set of known values of a vari-
able, an interpolating polynomial passing though them can be found. The approximation to the integral
of the input data is computed as the (analytic) integral of the considered interpolating polynomial. With
this methodology it is possible to mathematically bound the truncation error in the approximation of
the integral. Regarding round-of error , there is an important difference between numerical differentia-
tion and integration methods. As reported by Mørken in [ 20], while the former are very sensitive, the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
tion and integration methods. As reported by Mørken in [ 20], while the former are very sensitive, the
later are very insensitive to round-off error . This is due to the fact that, unlike the case of numerical
differentiation, numerical integration techniques usually do not entail the subtraction of nearly identical
quantities that might lead to dramatic losses of significance in the results of the calculations.
An example common approach, explained by Levy in [ 24], to derive quadrature expressions for the
generic integral

The quadratures given by Equation 3.15 are exact for polynomials of degree ≤ 𝑛.
There is a huge amount of bibliography about this topic, where many different approximations for
integrals can be found. Only the ones that have been implemented in the software code developed for
this study are included here below for completeness (Equations 3.17 and 3.18). They are called Trape-
zoidal and Simpson’s quadrature methods, respectively, and can be derived by applying the explained

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
zoidal and Simpson’s quadrature methods, respectively, and can be derived by applying the explained
methodology (see Equations 3.12 to 3.16) letting 𝑛 = 1 and 𝑛 = 2 , respectively.
• Trapezoidal quadrature: this method approximates the function on each interval as the straight
line (interpolating polynomial of order 1) that passes through the known data points at both ends
of the interval. Its expression is given by Eq. 3.17, obtained by letting 𝑛 = 1 in the explained
process for the derivation of quadrature approximations. It is called the trapezoidal rule since the
area of a trapezoid is the average height times the base [ 21]. Figure 3.2-a can serve to illustrate
how this method works.

• Simpson’s quadrature: this method approximates the function on every two consecutive intervals
with a parabola (interpolating polynomial of order 2) passing trough their three points. Its ex-
pression is given by Eq. 3.18, obtained by letting 𝑛 = 2 in the explained process for the derivation
of quadrature expressions. Figure 3.2-b can serve to illustrate how this method works.

Figure 3.2: Numerical quadrature techniques. In blue the function whose integral is being approximated. The actual approxi-
mations are in dashed, black line. a) Trapezoidal, b) Simpson’s. [ 21]
For better accuracy, the interval [𝑎, 𝑏] can be sequentially divided into smaller pieces (sub-intervals)
and the quadrature method can be applied to each of them (see Figures 3.3 and 3.4) in a process
known as composite quadrature.
Simpson’s rule, however , has a peculiarity when applying composite quadrature: it is applied to
adjacent couples of sub-intervals (see Figure 3.4) instead of individually to each of them. This is
because each interpolating parabola, passing through its three consecutive data points, spans two

because each interpolating parabola, passing through its three consecutive data points, spans two
neighbouring sub-intervals. [ 20]
Figure 3.3: The trapezoidal rule with one sub-interval (a) and five sub-intervals (b). [ 20]
Detailed information about the error analysis of these and many other integral approximations
is easily accessible in specialised bibliography. Regarding the two implemented approximations, the
(composite) trapezoidal method is second-order accurate (i.e., its truncation error is proportional to ℎኼ)
while the (composite) Simpson’s rule is fourth-order accurate (truncation error proportional to ℎኾ). The
inequalities that bound the error in the approximation of the integrals given by these two numerical
methods are included here below for completeness. [ 19] [ 24]
For any continuous function 𝑓(𝑥) whose two first derivatives are also continuous all along the
interval [𝑎, 𝑏], the truncation error , 𝐸(𝑓), in the trapezoidal rule with 𝑛 sub-intervals of length ℎ is given
by Equation 3.19.

On the other hand, the boundary for the error 𝐸(𝑓) when applying Simpson’s quadrature to a
continuous function 𝑓 whose four first derivatives are also continuous all along the interval [𝑎, 𝑏] with

It is important to note that, while the error of the approximation given by the trapezoidal rule is
proportional to ℎኼ, the error of the approximation to the integral given by Simpson’s rule is proportional
to ℎኾ (these exponents indicate the order of accuracy of the approximations). In other words, the error
in Simpson’s approximations to integrals decreases significantly faster than the error of trapezoidal
approximations as the length of the sub-intervals ℎ decreases.
3.2. Performance analysis strategy
Once the two different data type conversion mathematical models for the computation of equivalent
closed-loop Doppler data from an open-loop Doppler data set have been presented, it is time to es-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
closed-loop Doppler data from an open-loop Doppler data set have been presented, it is time to es-
tablish a strategy based on which to assess the conversion performances of such data type conversion
methodologies. That is the goal of this chapter .
The designed performance analysis strategy consists of the following steps:
1. Implementing in Matlab the two studied data type conversion models so that, given as input a
range and open-loop Doppler data set, it is converted into equivalent closed-loop Doppler data.
The software development effort carried out for this study is explained in Chapter 4.
2. Selecting the most convenient parameter settings to be configured along the whole data type
conversion performance analysis process, so that the two studied conversion models are tested
under a convenient, wide and varied set of circumstances covering realistic variations of the
representative problem by which this study is inspired. This is a key step for performing a simple
but good and complete analysis of the conversion capabilities of the studied conversion models.
The mission that inspires the experimental setup for this study consists of tracking a Mars orbiter

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
The mission that inspires the experimental setup for this study consists of tracking a Mars orbiter
from the Earth (as, for example, Mars Express).
3. Generating range and open-loop Doppler data sets to be given as input at the data conversion
phase.
4. Computing a reference (called real in this report - ℎ፜,፫) closed-loop Doppler observable against
which to compare the equivalent (called computed in this report - ℎ፜) closed-loop Doppler obser-
vations obtained from the data type conversion algorithms. The considered reference corresponds
to the classic model for processing closed-loop Doppler data given by Equation 4.2. [ 18]
5. Running the developed software that applies the data type conversion algorithms to an input
open-loop Doppler data set, giving as output equivalent closed-loop Doppler data.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
3.2. Performance analysis strategy 17
6. Comparing the computed closed-loop Doppler data ℎ፜ against the previously obtained reference
closed-loop Doppler data ℎ፜,፫ to assess the quality of the executed data type conversion. For
this end, several performance figures aimed at characterising the nature of that comparison are
calculated. Section 4.2 contains an explanation of how this comparison is carried out.
7. Concluding about the conversion performance/accuracy achieved by each of the two different
data type conversion methodologies studied, as a function of the configuration parameters of
the mathematical models and the geometric features of the simulated planetary geometries from
which the input open-loop Doppler data sets are generated.
For a good understanding of how this performance analysis strategy has been carried out, it is
convenient to clarify the meaning of two terms that are extensively used in this report from this point
onward:
• Scenario: this term is used to refer to a specific simulated planetary geometry based on which
range and open-loop Doppler data has been generated. From the same scenario, different data

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
range and open-loop Doppler data has been generated. From the same scenario, different data
sets have been obtained, being the only difference the time separation between consecutive
observations (𝑡፨). From the same scenario, different tests have been executed.
• T est: this term is used to refer to the execution of the data type conversion with a specific input
data set and with a specific combination of configuration settings for the data type conversion
algorithms. By varying the configuration settings of the data type conversion algorithms and the
nature of the input data sets, many different tests have been executed. Several tests have been
executed based on input data sets generated from the same scenario.
Section 3.2.1 explains the two different methodologies that have been used for the generation of the
range and open-loop Doppler data sets (consisting of discrete observations for a number of equidistant
times). Section 3.2.2, explains the reasoning to decide the most convenient combination of conversion
model’s parameter settings and geometries of the simulated scenarios aiming at a good, complete

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
model’s parameter settings and geometries of the simulated scenarios aiming at a good, complete
analysis of the conversion capabilities of the two studied conversion models. The outcome of this task
is the definition of a set of tests (presented in Section 3.2.5), covering a range of situations as wide
and varied as possible which with the performance of the conversation algorithms will be examined.
The purpose of this task is, based on the designed set of tests, to be able to gain some insight into the
influence of some of the parameters of the problem on the performance of the conversion models. T o
understand this section, it must be clear that there are different sorts of features that will potentially
impact on the performance of the models:
• On the one hand, the inherent configuration parameters of each of the conversion models.
• On the other hand, the nature of the input data set determined by the geometry of the simulated
scenarios.
Section 3.2.3, after having explained how the data sets have been generated (in Section 3.2.1)
and how the set of tests has been designed (in Section 3.2.2), is aimed at providing some more

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
and how the set of tests has been designed (in Section 3.2.2), is aimed at providing some more
detailed highlights for a deep understanding of how the performance analysis strategy of the data type
conversion models has been carried out. Section 3.2.4 explains the figures of merit calculated for every
executed test that allow direct comparisons of the performance of the models in different tests, as well
as the comparison of the performance each of the conversion models against each other for analogous
tests. Finally, Section 3.2.5 summarises and classifies the whole set of tests executed along this study.
3.2.1. Scenarios simulation and data sets generation
The two different applied procedures for the generation of range and open-loop Doppler observations
to be given as input for the data type conversion algorithms are the following:
• Simplistic analytical scenarios : the data sets are generated, in a simplistic way, from analytical
expressions that express the range (Eq. 3.21) and range-rate (Eq. 3.22) of an orbiter in a static
circular orbit (the central body does not move with respect to the fixed reference point) with

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
circular orbit (the central body does not move with respect to the fixed reference point) with
respect to a fixed reference point in space (the ground station) as a function of time.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
18 3. Doppler data type conversion
Regardless of its simplicity, this approach has been very useful during the performance analysis
of the models for quick generation of data sets with different parameter settings as well as for
assessing the quality of the numerical derivatives of the range (by comparing them with the
analytic ones that are available thanks to the usage of an analytic expression for the calculation
of the range observable) that play an crucial role in the corrective terms model (Section 3.1.2).
• Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat : the data sets are generated, in a more realistic way (in-
cluding third bodies and relative motion of the central body of the orbiter with respect to the
ground station, for example), simulating open-loop Doppler tracking data in scenarios simulated
by making use of Tudat.
The geometric parameters of the scenarios have been configured based on the representative
mission by which this study is inspired: tracking of a Mars orbiter from the Earth, as for example Mars
Express. Both approaches for the generation of the input data sets simulate this mission.
Simplistic analytical scenarios

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
Express. Both approaches for the generation of the input data sets simulate this mission.
Simplistic analytical scenarios
The importance of these scenarios lies on the fact that, since the time evolution of the range is computed
from an analytic expression, it is possible to calculate its derivatives analytically. This is of huge help
because it allows assessing the goodness/quality of the numerical derivatives of the range, making the
optimisation of the numerical differentiation settings possible and simple. It also helps to identify the
sort of errors that are present in the computed closed-loop Doppler observable. The main features of
these scenarios are the following:
• The zero-mass orbiter orbits its central body in circular orbits
• The central body of the orbiter does not move with respect to the ground station, which is
considered as a fixed point in space.
• The range and range-rate observations are computed, respectively, as the norm of the position
vector of the orbiter with respect to the ground station and its first time derivative.
• The ground station and the orbit may be co-planar or there might be a constant angle between

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
• The ground station and the orbit may be co-planar or there might be a constant angle between
the orbital plane and the vector connecting the centre of the orbit and the ground station.
• The only celestial bodies considered in this kind of scenarios are the Earth (as a fixed point
in space, coincident with the ground station), Mars (whose position vector with respect to the
Earth is constant) and the orbiter , that orbits Mars in unperturbed circular orbits. No third body
perturbations are considered. The only force acting on the orbiter is the gravitational acceleration
due to its central body.
• Constant point mass is the gravitational potential model considered for the central body. The
value considered for the gravitational parameter of Mars is 4.28277254 · 10ኻኽ 𝑚ኽ/𝑠ኼ.
Figure 3.5 shows the geometry of these kind of scenarios and helps understanding the derivation of the
analytic expressions for the range ( 𝑠, equation 3.21) and range-rate ( ̇ 𝑠, equation 3.22) as a function
of time ( 𝑡) that are shown here below for reference.

Figure 3.5: Geometric scheme for the derivation of the analytic expressions of the range ( ፬) and range-rate ( ̇፬) in simplistic

The simplistic analytic scenarios have been used for testing the sensitivity of the data type con-
version models to their inherent configuration parameters as well as for testing the sensitivity of the
data type conversion models to variations in three specific geometric features of the scenarios: the
semi-major axis (radius for circular orbits) of the orbit ( 𝑎), the interplanetary distance between the
ground station and the central body of the orbit ( Δ𝐷) and inclination angle of the orbital plane with
respect to the vector connecting the ground station to the centre of the orbit ( 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ).
A huge table specifying the configuration settings of all the tests performed along this study can be

Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package

metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
consulted in Appendix A.
Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat
Tudat, the TU Delft Astrodynamics T oolbox is a set of C++ software libraries developed and maintained
by staff and students in the Astrodynamics & Space Missions research group at the Faculty of Aerospace
Engineering of Delft University of T echnology (Netherlands), whose purpose is to provide users with
functionality to be able to simulate astrodynamics applications.
Making use of this software tool, several scenarios with different geometric settings have been
simulated. The importance of these scenarios lies on the need of testing the models under the most
realistic conditions possible. All the scenarios simulated in Tudat share, among others, the following
commonalities:
• The celestial bodies included in the scenario are: the Sun, the Earth, the Moon and Mars.
• The zero-mass orbiter is orbiting Mars.
• The ground station that simulates range and range-rate measurements via radio signals is fixed
to a point on the Earth’s surface, on the Equator , and rotates with Earth’s spin.
• The ephemeris of the considered celestial bodies are real, being the start of the simulations set
to the J2000 epoch.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
• The ephemeris of the considered celestial bodies are real, being the start of the simulations set
to the J2000 epoch.
• Range and range-rate observations are computed every 𝑡፨ seconds (1, 2 or 5s depending on the
test case) along the simulated time span (a complete orbit).
• The only accelerations acting on the orbiter are the central accelerations caused by gravitational
interaction with all the considered celestial bodies in the scenarios.
• The gravitational potential model for all the considered celestial bodies is constant point mass .
The baseline values for the eccentricity, inclination, right ascension of the ascending node and
argument of the perigee in the scenarios simulated in Tudat is zero, while the baseline value for the
semi-major axis ( 𝑎) is the same as for the analytical scenarios: 𝑎፛ፚ፬፞፥።፧፞ = 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚. The
values of some of these parameters have been varied to originate the set of tests for the sensitivity
analysis of the performance of the data type conversion models to the geometry of the simulated
scenarios, as explained in section 3.2.2.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
20 3. Doppler data type conversion
The realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat have been used the sensitivity of the data type conversion
models to their inherent configuration parameters as well as for testing the sensitivity of the data type
conversion models to variations in eccentricity ( 𝑒), inclination ( 𝑖) and right ascension of the ascending
node ( 𝑅𝐴𝐴𝑁).
A huge table specifying the configuration settings of all the tests performed along this study can be
consulted in Appendix A.
3.2.2. Sensitivity/parametric analysis
The cornerstone of the performance analysis strategy of the data type conversion mathematical models
is the analysis of the sensitivity of their conversion performance to variations in the the parameters
that play a role in the problem. However , testing individually the influence of varying every single
parameter of the models on their performance becomes impractical as soon as the amount of involved
parameters increases due to the fast growth of the number of possible combinations of parameter
values to be tested. In addition, the higher amount of tests, the bigger the amount of results to

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
values to be tested. In addition, the higher amount of tests, the bigger the amount of results to
analyse and conclude about, making the choice of the parameters of interest in the problem a decision
of crucial importance.
In this case, all the parameters involved in the experimental setup for testing the conversion per-
formance of the two investigated data type conversion models can be grouped in two clearly dissimilar
groups:
• Inherent parameters of the models : these are parameters that are part of the models themselves
like, for example, ΔT . The formulation of both models include this parameter . For both models,
their inherent configuration parameters as well as their set of selected values for the sensitivity
analysis are presented later in this section.
• Configuration parameters of the simulated scenarios : since the investigated data type conversion
models include numerical integration and differentiation of the input observables, it is evident that
the nature of the input data will impact the performance of the models. Several scenarios with
different orbital geometries have been simulated to assess the performance of the models with

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
different orbital geometries have been simulated to assess the performance of the models with
a wide range of input data sets generated from the simulation of various scenario geometries.
These parameters, as well as the set of selected combinations of values used for their sensitivity
analysis are presented later in this section.
Inherent parameters of the models
As commented before, these are parameters that are part of the models themselves and are included
in their formulations. Some of these parameters are common to both mathematical models and others
are specific of only one of them.
Parameters common to both data type conversion models:
• ΔT: time step between two consecutive conversions of input open-loop Doppler data into equiv-
alent closed-loop Doppler observations. It represents the time-span along which the input open-
loop Doppler observable is integrated according to equation 3.1 for the computation of the equiv-
alent closed-loop Doppler observations. For the sensitivity analysis it has been given the values:
10, 30, 60, 90 and 120 seconds.
• Time-step between consecutive input open-loop Doppler observations ( 𝑡፨): this parameter is not

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
10, 30, 60, 90 and 120 seconds.
• Time-step between consecutive input open-loop Doppler observations ( 𝑡፨): this parameter is not
strictly an inherent parameter of the models, nor it is a geometric feature of the scenarios. How-
ever , since (according to the results presented in Chapter 5) this parameter mostly impacts the
quality of the numerical integration and differentiation that are key aspects of the models them-
selves, it has been decided to include it in this group. For its sensitivity analysis, it has been given
the values 1, 2 and 5 seconds.
The parameter values for the sensitivity analysis of the performance of the two data type con-
version models to variations in each of these two parameters have been selected trying to cover
the range of values of maximum interest for the problem.
Parameters specific of each data type conversion model:

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
3.2. Performance analysis strategy 21
• Integration-based model:
– Numerical integration/quadrature technique : trapezoidal and Simpson’s are the two numer-
ical integration/quadrature techniques that have been tested. An introduction to these tech-
niques is included in Section 3.1.4.
• Corrective terms model :
– Highest order derivative included in the summation ( 𝑚): or , equivalently, amount of terms
included in the summation. This parameter refers to the amount of terms included in the
summation for the computation of the equivalent closed-loop Doppler data (Equation 3.4).
The analytic expression includes a summation of an infinite amount of terms, that obviously,
for its implementation and computation in a computer must be truncated. Thinking of this
parameter as amount of terms of the summation or as maximum (highest) derivative order
included in the summation is equivalent (every additional term includes a derivative of an
order higher than the previous one). This parameter determines the truncation error level
of the conversion model due to the truncation of the T aylor series (its analytic formulation

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of the conversion model due to the truncation of the T aylor series (its analytic formulation
includes infinite terms) that is present in its formulation, given by Equation 3.4.
The influence of this parameter has been investigated for values up to 6. More specifi-
cally, this parameter has been set to 6 (this number turned out to be enough based on
experience) for every test of the corrective terms conversion model, and consequently no
sensitivity analysis has been performed analysing thoroughly the impact of variations in this
parameter . Since all along this whole study the interest is more about understanding the
best achievable performances of the models rather than the computational efficiency in the
implementation of their algorithms, the analysis of this parameter has been considered of
secondary priority. However , for every test of the corrective terms data type conversion
model, the contribution of each sequentially added term (of the 6 included terms) in the
summation has been monitored. This has served to verify that, as expected from a T aylor
series expansion, the higher the number of terms included, the higher the accuracy of the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
series expansion, the higher the number of terms included, the higher the accuracy of the
achieved approximation. It has been observed that every single added extra term of the
summation can potentially improve the computed ℎ፜ observable, unless other sources of er-
ror start playing a role. Figure 3.6 can serve as an example of the discussed phenomenon,
showing the convergence of the four first terms of the summation corresponding to a test
of the baseline realistic scenario simulated in Tudat with 𝑡፨ = 1𝑠 and Δ𝑇 = 60𝑠 (test 348).
Figure 3.6: Example of monitoring how each sequential term of the summation in Eq. 3.4 contributes to improve the accuracy in
the computed observable ፡ᑔ when applying the corrective terms conversion model. Each of the 4 subplots shows an individual
term of the summation, in red, and the error (point by point difference ፡ᑔ,ᑣ ዅ፡ ᑔ), in blue, of the computed observable ፡ᑔ if adding
in the summation up to that term. Data set corresponding to a realistic scenario simulated in Tudat with ፞ ዆ ። ዆ ፑፀፀፍ ዆ ኺ ,
፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ and ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ . T est: 348.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
22 3. Doppler data type conversion
Nonetheless, it must be noted that it does not make sense to think of setting this parameter
to a much higher value unless the accuracy in the computations of ℎ፜,፫ and the numerical
derivatives is improved accordingly. Otherwise, for example, in the comparison of ℎ፜ against
ℎ፜,፫, the error in the computation of the latter would hide the error level in the computation of
ℎ፜ regardless of the amount of terms included in the summation. In other words, improving
the quality of the computed observable ℎ፜ by including more terms in the summation does
not make sense if the achieved level of accuracy in ℎ፜ is better than that in the computation
of ℎ፜,፫. It is only worth until ℎ፜ reaches the order of accuracy of ℎ፜,፫.
On the other hand, including a high amount of terms would imply the computation of very
high order numerical derivatives that might lead to other issues, as for example dealing with
round-off error . For a better understanding of this explanation, the discussion in Section 4.2
about how the quality of the computed observable is assessed might be helpful.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
about how the quality of the computed observable is assessed might be helpful.
– Stencil for the numerical derivatives : this refers to the scheme, approximation or mathe-
matical algorithm/expression applied for the numerical computation of the derivatives of the
range. It determines the truncation error of the computed numerical derivatives.
Fourth order central differences (see Equations 3.6 to 3.11) is the stencil applied for all the
computed numerical derivatives (up to the sixth) in all the tests performed for the corrective
terms data type conversion model.
Originally, a second order central differences stencil was applied. However , the error level
(point by point difference ℎ፜,፫ − ℎ፜) achieved by the model in the data type conversion was
of the order of the error in the computation of the first derivative (highest error in magnitude
of all order derivatives), being insufficient to reach the target accuracy levels. That is why it
was decided to apply a higher order scheme. The error (maximum point by point difference
of the numerically computed and the analytic derivative in the baseline simplistic analytic

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of the numerically computed and the analytic derivative in the baseline simplistic analytic
scenario) in the computation of the numerical derivatives of the range by applying both
second- and fourth-order stencils were compared against each other and the fourth-order
one showed significantly better performances as shown in Figures 3.10 and 3.9, especially
in the error levels of the first and second derivatives of the range, which are the ones of
highest magnitude (as shown in Figure 5.9) in the summation for the computation of ℎ፜
(given by Eq. 3.4).
Figures 3.7 and 3.8 support the decision of upgrading the stencil from second order to fourth
order central differences. Figure 3.7 shows the maximum error in the computation of ℎ፜ with
input data sets corresponding to the baseline simplistic analytical scenario (defined in Section
3.2.1) for several values of the parameter Δ𝑇, together with the target accuracy of 20% the
expected accuracy of the instrumentation (in orange) and the expected accuracy of the
instrumentation itself (in red). The dark blue line corresponds to the maximum error in the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
instrumentation itself (in red). The dark blue line corresponds to the maximum error in the
data type conversion when the term ℎ፨ (first derivative of the range) is calculated analytically
and the other derivatives of the range are computed numerically by applying second order
central differences (the normal case, since ℎ፨ would be an input in real life). The light blue
line represents the maximum error in the data type conversion when the term ℎ፨ and the
summation term of index 𝑖 = 2 (the one including the second derivative of the range) are
calculated analytically and the other derivatives of the range are computed numerically by
applying second order central differences. Finally, the green line represents the maximum
error in the data type conversion if all the derivatives are calculated analytically. Based on
the analysis of the three last explained lines of the plot, it was concluded that it was needed
to improve the accuracy in the computation of the numerical derivatives. Figure 3.8 shows
the same analysis as the one presented in Figure 3.7 but corresponding to the application
of the fourth order central differences stencil. It is interesting to note in Figure 3.8 that the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of the fourth order central differences stencil. It is interesting to note in Figure 3.8 that the
error in the computed observable ℎ፜ when only the term ℎ፨ (first derivative of the range)
is calculated analytically (blue line) is very similar to the error in the computed observable
ℎ፜ when all the derivatives of the range are computed analytically. This fact led to the
conclusion that it is not worth upgrading to the higher order stencil.

of ፡ᑔ (point by point difference ፡ᑔ,ᑣ ዅ፡ ᑔ) with input data sets corresponding to the baseline simplistic analytical scenario (defined
in Section 3.2.1) for several values of the parameter ጂፓ , together with the target accuracy of 20% of the expected accuracy of the
instrumentation (in orange) and the expected accuracy of the instrumentation itself (in red). The dark blue line corresponds to
the maximum conversion error when the term ፡ᑠ (first derivative of the range) is calculated analytically and the other derivatives
of the range are computed numerically by applying second order central differences (the normal case, since ፡ᑠ would be an

of index ። ዆ ኼ (the one including the second derivative of the range) are calculated analytically and the other derivatives of the

range are computed numerically by applying second order central differences. Finally, the green line represents the maximum

of ፡ᑔ (point by point difference ፡ᑔ,ᑣ ዅ፡ ᑔ) with input data sets corresponding to the baseline simplistic analytical scenario (defined
in Section 3.2.1) for several values of the parameter ጂፓ , together with the target accuracy of 20% of the expected accuracy of
the instrumentation (in orange). The blue line corresponds to the maximum conversion error when the term ፡ᑠ (first derivative
of the range) is calculated analytically and the other derivatives of the range are computed numerically by applying fourth order

of the range) is calculated analytically and the other derivatives of the range are computed numerically by applying fourth order
