error in the data type conversion if all the derivatives of the range are calculated analytically.
This feature of the model has not been submitted to a sensitivity analysis because it was
considered not to be a priority since the first tries (second- and fourth-order central differ-
ences) were enough to find a stencil capable of providing numerical derivatives of the range
(in simplistic analytic scenarios) with accuracy enough so that the error in the converted

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
24 3. Doppler data type conversion
Doppler data ℎ፜ is smaller than that in the computation of ℎ፜,፫. For a better understanding
of the last comment, the discussion in Section 4.2 about how the quality of the computed
observable is assessed might be helpful.
One last important note about the study for the selection of the numerical differentiation
stencil is that, all along the study, for the two considered stencils and for each order derivative
the parameter ℎ (see Equations 3.6 to 3.11) was set to its experimentally optimum value,
obtained as explained in the following bullet point.
– Time step for each numerical derivative order ( 𝛿𝑡፧ ): this refers to the time separation be-
tween two consecutive data points (being all of them equispaced) involved in the stencil,
scheme or mathematical algorithm/expression applied for the numerical approximation of
the derivatives of the range. It has a crucial effect in the total error of the computed nu-
merical derivatives because it determines their round-off error level and it also affects the
truncation error of the numerical differentiation stencil.
This feature of the corrective terms conversion model has not been strictly submitted to

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
truncation error of the numerical differentiation stencil.
This feature of the corrective terms conversion model has not been strictly submitted to
a sensitivity analysis, but is has been optimised for the baseline simplistic analytical sce-
nario (defined in Section 3.2.1) and kept constant for all the tests. Its optimisation was
accomplished, for each derivative order , by looking for the time step that led to the smallest
maximum error along a complete orbit when comparing, point by point, every order nu-
merical derivative against its analytic counterpart. The optimum configured values are, in
ascendant order from the first to the sixth derivatives of the range: 30, 120, 160, 340, 400
and 600 seconds, respectively.
As an example of how this study has been performed, Figure 3.9 shows the maximum
error along a complete orbit in the computation of the four first derivatives of the range for
the baseline simplistic analytical scenario by applying fourth order central differences with
several values of the parameter 𝛿𝑡፧ (equivalent to ℎ in Equations 3.6 to 3.11). Figure 3.10
shows the analogous study for the case of applying second order central differences, which

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
shows the analogous study for the case of applying second order central differences, which
was the first tested numerical differentiation stencil, as explained in the previous bullet point.
Figure 3.9: Optimisation of the time step ᎑፭ᑟ (or ፡) for the four first numerical derivatives of the range by applying fourth
order central differences. Subplots a, b, c and d show the evolution of the maximum error in the first, second, third and fourth
derivatives of the range, respectively, along a complete orbit in the baseline simplistic analytic scenario (defined in Section 3.2.1).
Figure 3.10: Optimisation of the time step ᎑፭ᑟ (or ፡) for the four first numerical derivatives of the range by applying second
order central differences. Subplots a, b, c and d show the evolution of the maximum error in the first, second, third and fourth
derivatives of the range, respectively, along a complete orbit in the baseline simplistic analytic scenario (defined in Section 3.2.1).

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
3.2. Performance analysis strategy 25
An interesting feature that can be observed in Figure 3.9 is the shape of the curve rep-
resenting the maximum error in the numerical derivatives as a function of the time step.
For every order derivative there is an optimum 𝛿𝑡 for which the error is minimum. For 𝛿𝑡
values smaller than the optimum, the maximum error worsens driven by an increase in the
round-off error present in the numerical derivatives. On the contrary, for 𝛿𝑡 values bigger
than the optimum, the maximum error worsens driven by an increase in the truncation error
present in numerical differentiation stencil. The opposite trends of round-off and truncation
errors in numerical differentiation are schematised in Figure 3.11 for completeness.
Figure 3.11: Schematic representation of the opposite trends of round-off and truncation errors in numerical differentiation as
a function of the time step.
The reason why this feature of the model has not been submitted to a sensitivity analysis is
that it is considered that the automatic/systematic optimisation of the numerical differenti-
ation settings for every orbital geometry (semi-major axis, eccentricity) is out of the scope

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
ation settings for every orbital geometry (semi-major axis, eccentricity) is out of the scope
of this study. In addition, since the input data sets used all along this study consist of eq-
uispaced discrete range and open-loop Doppler observations, for good performance of the
numerical differentiation settings it is required that there are data points of the input data
set at the specific times required by the numerical differentiation stencils to compute the
derivatives of the range. For this reason, the optimum time step for every numerical deriva-
tive order ( 𝛿𝑡፧ ) and the different values for the time separation between consecutive input
data observations ( 𝑡፨) have been conveniently set so that every configured optimum value
of 𝛿𝑡፧ is at the same time exactly divisible by 1, 2 and 5 that are the tested values for the
parameter 𝑡፨. This has allowed the matching of the available data points in the input data
sets with the times at which input observations are required by the numerical differentiation
stencil.
However , in an attempt to gain deeper insight into the implications of this parameter on the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
stencil.
However , in an attempt to gain deeper insight into the implications of this parameter on the
performance of the data type conversion model, some extra analysis was performed. It was
aimed at finding a systematic approach to tune optimally 𝛿𝑡፧ having in mind that, according
to the results of the sensitivity analysis of the corrective terms model to the geometry of
the simulated scenario (presented in Section 5.2.2), its conversion performance worsens
significantly whenever the orbital velocity surpasses the circular orbital velocity for which
the numerical differentiation settings where optimised (that corresponding to the baseline
simplistic analytic scenario).
The idea to face this problem consisted of, depending on the orbital geometry of the scenario
on which the test to be performed is based, finding (from basic astrodynamics expressions)
the maximum (worst case) orbital velocity along the whole orbit (at the perigee for eccentric
orbits and constant - but dependant on the semimajor axis - for circular orbits) and re-
tune the numerical differentiation settings following the same procedure that was applied

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
tune the numerical differentiation settings following the same procedure that was applied
originally but for a new circular orbit whose orbital velocity (radius or equivalently, semi-
major axis) equals the maximum (worst case) orbital velocity expected in the scenario on
which the test to be performed is based. The logic behind this approach is to set the
numerical differentiation settings in such a way that the numerical differentiation settings
are able to capture the faster dynamics (compared to the baseline simplistic analytic scenario

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
26 3. Doppler data type conversion
based on which the numerical differentiation settings were optimised) of the the orbit of the
scenario on which the test to be performed is based. This analysis was abandoned because
the results obtained were not satisfactory enough. However , some interesting conclusions
were made from it:
⋄ Even though by applying this approach the maximum error in the computed observable
along a complete orbit is significantly reduced (sometimes by more than 1 order of
magnitude), if the deviation from the baseline settings of the semimajor axis (reduction)
or eccentricity (increase) is significant (more than 20% of A or 𝑒 ≥ 0.2 approximately for
the few analysed cases) the maximum error still keeps clearly above the target accuracy
levels (specified in Section 3.2.4). On the contrary, for deviations smaller than those
thresholds, the error along a complete orbit can be of the order of the accuracy targets.
⋄ In addition, it has been observed that in the case of eccentric orbits, while the error in the
computed observable clearly improves (sometimes by more than 1 order of magnitude)

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
computed observable clearly improves (sometimes by more than 1 order of magnitude)
in the vicinity of the perigee (fastest orbital velocity) the error level along the rest of
the orbit (slower orbital velocity) is deteriorated (increases by a factor of several units)
compared to the case of the observable being computed by applying the numerical
differentiation settings that were optimised based on the baseline simplistic analytic
scenario. It is thought that it is due to the increased importance of round-off error
when 𝛿𝑡፧ is reduced aiming at capturing faster orbital dynamics.
⋄ A new approach to face this issue could be trying the implement 𝛿𝑡፧ in a self-adjusting
way so that it takes different (for eccentric orbits) optimum values along the orbit de-
pending on the local orbital velocity. However , if would require a continuous input
Doppler observable or a strategy to solve the potential mismatch between the available
data points and the times at which the numerical differentiation stencils require data.
Geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios
From all the parameters that play a role in the configuration of the simulated scenarios, only those

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
Geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios
From all the parameters that play a role in the configuration of the simulated scenarios, only those
considered to be more significant from the point of view of the performance assessment of the con-
version models have been included in the sensitivity analysis for practical purposes and for simplicity
of the study as a whole.
Included in the sensitivity analysis: the following are the geometric parameters of the simulated
scenarios whose values have been varied from its baseline configuration in order to assess their indi-
vidual effect on the conversion performance of the studied data type conversion models.
• Orbit eccentricity ( 𝑒): the values tested in its sensitivity analysis are 0, 0.4 and 0.8.
• Orbit inclination angle: in the simplistic analytic scenarios, denoted as ( 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ), it has been given
the values 0, 30 and 60 degrees. In the realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat, denoted as ( 𝑖), it
as been given the values 0, 45 and 90 degrees.
• Right ascension of the ascending node ( 𝑅𝐴𝐴𝑁): it as been tested with the values 0, 45 and 90
degrees.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
• Right ascension of the ascending node ( 𝑅𝐴𝐴𝑁): it as been tested with the values 0, 45 and 90
degrees.
• Semi-major axis ( 𝑎): it has been tested with its baseline value 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚 and also with
the values 0.5 · 𝐴 and 3 · 𝐴.
• Interplanetary distance between the ground station and the central body of the orbit ( Δ𝐷): it has
been tested with its baseline value 𝐷 = 2.25 · 10 ኻኻ𝑚 and also with the values ፃ
ኻኺኺ and ፃ
ኻኺኺኺ .
The values for the sensitivity analysis to each of these parameters have been selected trying to cover
the range of values of most interest for the problem.
A huge table specifying the configuration settings of all the tests performed along this study can be
consulted in Appendix A.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
3.2. Performance analysis strategy 27
Excluded from the sensitivity analysis
• Third orbital orientation angle (argument of the perigee). This is the angle that determines the
orientation of the orbit in its plane. For this reason, it is expected not to have a significant impact
on the performance of any of the two studied data type conversion models and that is why this
parameter has been considered of secondary importance.
• Other scenario features: number of considered celestial bodies, gravitational potential models,
perturbations (atmospheric drag, solar radiation pressure, etc.) and every other parameter not
explicitly mentioned in this section. These parameters have been considered of secondary impor-
tance because for this initial study of the capabilities of the two mathematical models the focus is
on on understanding how the main geometric features of the scenarios impact the performances
of the models rather than on executing highly realistic simulations.
As commented before, the fact of not including more parameters in the sensitivity analysis or testing
more values for each parameter is to avoid dealing with too much data and to try to keep the simplicity

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
more values for each parameter is to avoid dealing with too much data and to try to keep the simplicity
of the study as a whole at a reasonable level.
3.2.3. Key aspects of the work flow
Once the data set generation process has been explained (in Section 3.2.1) and the two groups of
parameters whose impact on the performance of the investigated data type conversion models is to
be analysed have been presented (in Section 3.2.2), this section is aimed at clarifying a few important
details about how the sensitivity/parametric analysis have been carried out. The following list specifies
some important aspects of the analysis process that are expected to provide the reader with a better
global comprehension of how the core study of this work has been executed.
• Both groups of parameters (inherent parameters of the data type conversion models and geomet-
ric parameters of the simulated scenarios) have been analysed independently from each other .
That is to say, keeping all the parameters of the other group constant and repeating the complete
set of tests setting the parameters of that other group to a few different specific configurations

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
set of tests setting the parameters of that other group to a few different specific configurations
for completeness and robustness of the conclusions. More specifically, the performance analysis
of the models to their inherent configuration parameters has been repeated for five different
scenario geometries, whereas the performance analysis of the models to the geometry of the
simulated scenarios has been repeated for two different settings of the inherent configuration
parameters of the models. This has simplified enormously the study as a whole by reducing the
amount of possible combinations of all the parameters whose impact on the performance of the
models has been analysed.
A huge table specifying the configuration settings of all the tests performed along this study can
be consulted in Appendix A.
• For each of the two data type conversion models and for their two performance sensitivity analysis
(to each of the two groups of parameters - inherent parameters of the conversion models and
geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios) data sets generated by simulating scenarios
in Tudat and also data sets generated by analytic expressions implemented in Matlab have been

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
in Tudat and also data sets generated by analytic expressions implemented in Matlab have been
tested for robustness of the conclusions.
• Both data type conversion models have been given as input identical data sets for every tested
combination of inherent parameters of the models and geometry of the simulated scenarios for
the purpose of performing consistent comparisons of the conversion performances achieved by
each model in different tests (Sections 5.1 and 5.2), as well as for consistent comparisons of the
conversion performances achieved by each model against each other in analogous tests (Chapter
5.3).
• The performances of both data type conversion models have been assessed following exactly
the same methodology (explained in Section 4.2) and calculating the same performance figures,
presented in Section 3.2.4, for the purpose of performing consistent comparisons of the conver-
sion performances achieved by each model in different tests (Sections 5.1 and 5.2), as well as
for consistent comparisons of the conversion performances achieved by each model against each
other in analogous tests (Chapter 5.3).

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
28 3. Doppler data type conversion
3.2.4. Figures of merit
The purpose of this section is to explain which and why are the performance indicators based on which
the goodness/quality of the observables computed by applying the investigated data type conversion
models has been assessed. They have been calculated for every test and every model to allow consis-
tent comparisons of the conversion performances achieved by each model in different tests (Sections
5.1 and 5.2), as well as consistent comparisons of the conversion performances achieved by each
model against each other in analogous tests (Chapter 5.3). The selected performance figures are the
following:
• Maximum error (absolute value) in the computed observable along a complete orbit. The cal-
culation of the maximum error has been performed by comparing the computed observable ℎ፜
(calculating the data point by data point difference) with respect to the reference observable
ℎ፜,፫ (the 𝑟 standing for real), obtained by application of the classic, well-known model used for
processing closed-loop Doppler data that is described by Equation 2.9.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
processing closed-loop Doppler data that is described by Equation 2.9.
Figures 4.7, 4.9 and 4.10 are example plots of how the error in the computed closed-loop Doppler
observable evolves along a complete orbit corresponding to different test cases.
• Percentage of compliant observations of the computed closed-loop Doppler observable along a
complete orbit for a target accuracy level of ±5% of the expected accuracy of the instruments
(target 1).
• Percentage of compliant observations of the computed closed-loop Doppler observable along a
complete orbit for a target accuracy level of ±20% of the expected accuracy of the instruments
(target 2).
• Percentage of compliant observations of the computed closed-loop Doppler observable along a
complete orbit for a target accuracy level of ±100% of expected accuracy of the instruments
(target 3).
The expected accuracy of the instruments 𝛿(Δ𝑇) is a function of Δ𝑇 (time interval between two
consecutive close-loop Doppler data conversions) and has been calculated from the relation given in
Equation 3.23
𝛿(Δ𝑇)[ 𝑚
𝑠 ] = 𝛿(60𝑠)[ 𝑚

Δ𝑇[𝑠] (3.23)

metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:

𝛿(Δ𝑇)[ 𝑚

and consequently the percentages of compliance of the errors of the models with the accuracy targets
are also a function of Δ𝑇. A graphical representation of how the expected accuracy of the instrumen-
tation evolves as a function of Δ𝑇 is included in Figure 3.8 (red line) together with the evolution of
the target 2 (±20% of the expected accuracy of the instruments) for the accuracy in the computed
closed-loop Doppler observable (yellow line). [ 25]
The selection of these parameters instead of any others is due to their simplicity and effectiveness
to express the nature of the error in the computed closed-loop Doppler observable. On the one hand,
the maximum error along a complete orbit gives an idea o what is the worst performance of the model
along a complete orbital period. On the other hand, the percentages of compliance with the accuracy
targets provide a deeper insight on how the magnitude of the error is distributed along the orbit.
The percentages of the accuracy targets have been set between ±5% and ±100% of the expected

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
The percentages of the accuracy targets have been set between ±5% and ±100% of the expected
accuracy of the instruments in order to analyse how big or small is the error of the observable obtained
by the application of the data type conversion models relative to the magnitude of the expected accuracy
of the instruments.
3.2.5. Test cases
According to the planned performance analysis strategy presented all along Section 3.2, the two in-
vestigated data type conversion models have been submitted to a certain amount of tests with many
different combinations of configuration settings.
As a summary, the integration-based model has been submitted to 270 tests (150 based on simplistic
analytic scenarios and 120 based on scenarios simulated in Tudat) for its sensitivity analysis to inherent

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
3.2. Performance analysis strategy 29
parameters of the model and 216 tests (108 based on simplistic analytic scenarios and 108 based on
scenarios simulated in Tudat) for its sensitivity analysis to the geometry of the simulated scenarios,
adding up to a subtotal of 486 tests.
On the other hand, the corrective-terms model has been submitted to 135 tests (75 based on
simplistic analytic scenarios and 60 based on scenarios simulated in Tudat) for its sensitivity analysis
to inherent parameters of the model and 108 tests (54 based on simplistic analytic scenarios and 54
based on scenarios simulated in Tudat) for its sensitivity analysis to the geometry of the simulated
scenarios, adding up to a subtotal of 243 tests.
T o document all the 729 tests which with the two conversion models have been examined, Appendix
A includes tables specifying the configuration settings of every single executed test.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
4
Conversion models development
In the previous chapters, the difference between open- and closed loop Doppler data types was ex-
plained (see Section 2.2). In addition, the need for data type conversion algorithms capable of trans-
lating a data type into the other was justified, and two such methodologies proposed by Dirkx et al. in
[7] were introduced (in Section 3.1). The strategy planned for analysing the conversion performance
of both techniques has also been discussed previously in this report (Section 3.2).
The purpose of this chapter is to explain the software development process carried out in order to
implement, validate and experiment with the two data type conversion mathematical models presented
in Section 3.1.
Section 4.1 explains the implementation of the developed software code, whereas Section 4.2 dis-
cusses the validation of both the implemented software and the two data type conversion models
whose conversion performances are the focus of this study.
After that, Chapter 5 presents the results of the experimentation carried out with the two inves-
tigated data type conversion models by executing many test cases with the software code whose

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
tigated data type conversion models by executing many test cases with the software code whose
development process is presented in this chapter .
4.1. Implementation
As explained in the performance analysis strategy (Section 3.2), each of the two studied data type
conversion mathematical models (presented in Section 3.1) has been submitted to two independent
sensitivity analysis: one to the inherent parameters of the model and the other to the geometric
features of the simulated scenarios. For this reason, four different scripts have been developed: one
per mathematical model and sensitivity analysis.
In addition, a very simple C++ script has been developed in order to generate the input data sets
for the tests cases based on scenarios simulated in Tudat. The development of this script will not be
explained since its purpose is limited to generation of the input data sets required for the execution of
those tests.
Subsections 4.1.1 and 4.1.2 present separately the implementation of the scripts developed for
testing each of the two data type conversion models.
4.1.1. Integration-based model

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
testing each of the two data type conversion models.
4.1.1. Integration-based model
The implementation of this mathematical model is explained schematically in Figures 4.1, 4.2 and 4.3.
Figure 4.1 schematises the implementation of the model for the sensitivity analysis of the performance
of the model to its inherent configuration parameters, while Figure 4.2 specifies how the core function
of the model is implemented. Figure 4.3 schematises the implementation of the model for the sensitivity
analysis of the performance of the model to the geometric features of the simulated scenarios.
31

32 4. Conversion models development
Sensitivity to the inherent configuration parameters
According to the performance analysis strategy explained in Section 3.2, the inherent configuration
parameters of the integration-based model that have been submitted to a sensitivity analysis are Δ𝑇
and 𝑡፨. That is why, after setting the constant parameters of the simulations, the code includes two
nested loops, each one running through the tested values for Δ𝑇 and 𝑡፨ as shown in Figure 4.1.
Figure 4.1: Implementation of the integration-based model. Software block-diagram for the sensitivity analysis of the perfor-
mance of the model to its inherent configuration parameters.
First, 𝑡፨ is given the corresponding value for the current iteration of its loop. Right after that, the
input data set is imported from files (generated either by Tudat or during the execution of tests of
simplistic analytic scenarios for the corrective-terms model). This is done before entering the Δ𝑇 loop
because the same data set (with the same 𝑡፨ setting) is used for tests with different Δ𝑇 values.
After that, the code enters the Δ𝑇 loop and that variable is assigned the value corresponding to the

metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
After that, the code enters the Δ𝑇 loop and that variable is assigned the value corresponding to the
current iteration of its loop. Once Δ𝑇 is set, the times ( 𝑡፡ᑔ) at which the closed-loop Doppler observable
(ℎ፜) is to be calculated are computed as well as the real (reference for assessment of the quality of
the observables computed by the model) closed-loop Doppler observable ℎ፜,፫ (it is computed at this
point only if it has not been imported from external files at the data import stage. When importing
data generated by Tudat the data set includes ℎ፜,፫. However , when importing data generated during
the execution of tests of analytic scenarios for the corrective-terms model, ℎ፜,፫ is not included in the
imported data set and it is computed at the indicated point of the code).

text:
4.1. Implementation 33
The next step is the call to the functions that actually compute the closed-loop Doppler observable
by implementing the algorithm of the mathematical model. The implementation of these functions
(they are two identical functions except for the fact that they apply different numerical quadrature
techniques), that are represented as a unique light blue block in Figure 4.1, is presented separately in
Figure 4.2.
Figure 4.2: Core function of the implementation of the integration-based model. Software block-diagram.
According to that scheme, the function consists of a loop that runs through all the times ( 𝑡፡ᑔ) at
which the observable ( ℎ፜) is to be computed. Then, for each of that times, the software selects all the
input open-loop Doppler observations that correspond to the Δ𝑇 time interval right before the current
𝑡፡ᑔ. Finally, the numerical quadrature (Simpson’s, S, and trapezoidal, T) functions are called for the
computation of the numerical integral of the selected input data along the corresponding time interval
Δ𝑇. This procedure is repeated for every 𝑡፡ᑔ of the inner loop.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package

metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:

Once the ℎ፜ observable is computed, its error (data point by data point difference with respect to
the real observable - ℎ፜,፫) is calculated and, based on that, the performance figures of the model for
the current test are calculated as well. At this point, the graphs and results are saved and that is the
end of the current test.
The same process is repeated for all the configured Δ𝑇 and 𝑡፨ values combinations.
Sensitivity to the geometry of the simulated scenarios
According to the performance analysis strategy explained in Section 3.2, three are the geometric param-
eters of the simulated scenarios that are included in the sensitivity analysis (note that these parameters
are different for simplistic analytic scenarios and for realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat). That is why,
after setting the constant parameters of the simulations, the code enters consecutively three nested
loops, each one running through the test values for each of the three analysed geometric parameters
of the simulated scenarios, as schematised in Figure 4.3.

of the simulated scenarios, as schematised in Figure 4.3.
Once in the inner loop, all the the parameters for the test corresponding to the current iteration are
already set and the input data set is imported from external files (generated either by Tudat or during
the execution of tests of analytic scenarios for the corrective-terms model).

4.2. Validation
Once the equivalent closed-loop Doppler observable has been obtained by applying the data type
conversion mathematical models to the input open-loop Doppler data set, it is of crucial importance to
assess the quality of the computed data. This serves two objectives. First, it serves as validation of
the two different mathematical approaches for the data type conversion process. Secondly, it serves
as validation of the implementation of the conversion algorithms.
Both validations have been executed at once by assessing the quality of the computed observable
(ℎ፜) as compared to (data point by data point difference) the reference observable obtained from the
application of the classic, well-known model used for processing closed-loop Doppler data ℎ፜,፫ (the 𝑟
standing for real) that is described by Equation 4.2 [18]
ℎ፜,፫(𝜏ኻ) = 1
Δ𝑇 (𝜎(𝜏ኻ) − 𝜎(𝜏ኻ − Δ𝑇)) (4.2)

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
standing for real) that is described by Equation 4.2 [18]
ℎ፜,፫(𝜏ኻ) = 1
Δ𝑇 (𝜎(𝜏ኻ) − 𝜎(𝜏ኻ − Δ𝑇)) (4.2)
where 𝜎 stands for the raw range observable and 𝜏 stands for the proper times recorded by an observer ,
as explained in Section 2.2.1.
Note that, even though for the validation process some discussion about the assessment of the
quality of the data type conversion results is needed as well as to look at some conversion results, the
content and the purpose of this section are completely different from those of Chapter 5, where the
obtained results for many different configuration settings are discussed.
Checking that the two data type conversion models are capable (under certain circumstances, as
discussed in Chapter 5) of achieving accuracy levels in the computed observable of the order of mag-
nitude of the target accuracies specified in Section 3.2.4 has been considered enough to assume both
validations to be successful.
Although this methodology might seem very simple at first glance, the analysis of the errors of
the models computed in this way is more complicated than it looks like at first sight. The complexity

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
the models computed in this way is more complicated than it looks like at first sight. The complexity
arises from the fact that the computation of ℎ፜,፫ (the reference against which the computed ℎ፜ is
compared to assess its quality) has also finite accuracy or , in other words, includes some error . Two
direct consequences of this fact are:

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
4.2. Validation 39
• When analysing the error of the outcomes of any of the investigated data type conversion models
computed as the point by point difference ℎ፜,፫ − ℎ ፜ along a complete orbit, it is not trivial to
conclude which of the two observables is responsible for the observed pattern. It depends on
which of them has a bigger error .
• The finiteness of the accuracy (due to round-off error) in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ sets a limit in
the quality assessment of the observables computed by the investigated data type conversion
models. This means that for the cases (if any) in which the level of error in the computation
of ℎ፜ by the conversion models is smaller than the level of error in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, the
obtained error of the conversion models calculated by applying this methodology will always have
(at least) the magnitude of the error level in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ (the bigger level of error
of the two compared observables), overriding the smaller error in the computation of ℎ፜ that is
then one of interest for this study.
For these reasons, and being it the reference against which the computed ℎ፜ is compared to as-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
then one of interest for this study.
For these reasons, and being it the reference against which the computed ℎ፜ is compared to as-
sess its level of uncertainty, it becomes of crucial importance to understand the error present in the
computation of ℎ፜,፫. There are three important aspects regarding the analysis of the error level in the
computation of ℎ፜,፫, that can be explained based on the analysis of Equation 4.2:
1. The lower the order of magnitude of the ranges, the better the level of accuracy in the computation
of ℎ፜,፫. This is due to the fact that computers store numbers with a fixed amount of digits,
thus limiting the accuracy to which real numbers can be represented. In addition, floating-point
arithmetic (the one used all along this study and by most conventional computer programs) is not
exact even if the individual terms are exactly represented. Some operations, like the difference
between two very big and similar numbers, significantly degrade the round-off error level of the
result because its only significant digits are those by which the two operands differed. This is the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
result because its only significant digits are those by which the two operands differed. This is the
case for the computation of the difference of two nearly identical ranges of very high order of
magnitude. [ 26]
As an example, working with double precision variables in Matlab (16 digits), the difference of
two ranges whose order of magnitude is 10ኻኻ would inevitably include uncertainty of order 10ዅ኿
([27]). Additional information about numerical noise as well as strategies to mitigate its effects
can be consulted in [ 6] and [ 28].
This fact has been observed in the sensitivity analysis of the performances of the models to the
variation of the interplanetary distance Δ𝐷 between the ground station and the central body of
the orbiter (simplistic analytical scenarios). T able 4.1 can serve as an example to illustrate this
phenomenon. It shows how the accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ improves (the maximum error
decreases) approximately by the same factor as the value of Δ𝐷 is decreased. Note that, in this
case, showing the maximum error along a complete orbit is enough because the observed error

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
case, showing the maximum error along a complete orbit is enough because the observed error
pattern is white noise. The reason why it is believed that the error patterns (white noise) obtained
for the particular tests to which the values of T able 4.1 refer correspond to the accuracy in the
computation of ℎ፜,፫ is explained in the next point. Note that the scenarios to which the values of
T able4.1 refer (reference analytical scenario with variations in Δ𝐷) were selected because it was
the baseline analytic scenario for which the numerical differentiation settings of the corrective-
terms model were optimised and it was expected that both data type conversion models should
achieve performances close to their best when tested against tests based on those scenarios.

text:
40 4. Conversion models development
2. The dominant error in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ is round-off error (its origin being the phenomenon
discussed in point 1 of this list), which is of random nature. On the other hand, as commented
later in in this Section, there is no reason to expect the error in the computation of ℎ፜ to be of
random nature (and inversely proportional to Δ𝐷), since it also includes truncation error . For
this reason, when the observed pattern of the error in the assessment of the conversion perfor-
mances of the models is of random nature bounded by symmetric extreme values of the order
of magnitude of the accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ (when possible, checking that the er-
ror level improves as Δ𝐷 is reduced would as strong argument as well), it is concluded that the
error in the computation of ℎ፜ by applying the data type conversion models is smaller than the
error in the computation of ℎ፜,፫. In these cases the level of error of ℎ፜ remains unknown, but
it is concluded that it is smaller than the level of error in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, that is known
(according to the explanation given in point 1 of this list). Figure 4.7 illustrates this phenomenon.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
(according to the explanation given in point 1 of this list). Figure 4.7 illustrates this phenomenon.
On the contrary, an example of the error in the computation of ℎ፜ not being smaller than the
error in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ is shown in Figure 4.9.
Figure 4.7: Closed-loop Doppler observable error along a complete orbit. Integration-based model with Simpson’s quadrature.
Baseline simplistic analytic scenario. ጂፓ ዆ ኻኼኺ፬ ; ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T est case: 10.
3. The error level in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ is inversely proportional to Δ𝑇. This is explained because
the uncertainty in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, according to Equation 4.2, comes from the uncertainty
in the range observations (of order ten raised to the negative power of the difference between
the amount of digits that the computer uses to store the data and the exponent of the order of
magnitude of the ranges) and, regardless of their uncertainty, their difference is divided by Δ𝑇
consequently decreasing the error level in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ by the same factor . Figure 4.8
illustrates this phenomenon.
It shows the maximum error in the computed observable along a complete orbit obtained by

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
illustrates this phenomenon.
It shows the maximum error in the computed observable along a complete orbit obtained by
applying the integration-based model with Simpson’s quadrature (whose measurable accuracy is
always -in the test cases carried out along this study- limited by the finiteness of the accuracy in
the computation of ℎ፜,፫ as explained in Chapter 5) to the same input data set (identical scenario
geometry) with several different Δ𝑇 settings. Note that showing the maximum error along a
complete orbit is enough because the observed error pattern is of random nature (bounded by
symmetric extreme values of the order of the accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫).

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
4.2. Validation 41
Figure 4.8: Maximum error along a complete orbit in the closed-loop Doppler observable computed by applying the integration-
based model with Simpson’s quadrature with identical input data set for several values of ጂፓ . Geometric settings corresponding
to the baseline simplistic analytic scenario. ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ .
On the other hand, the computation of ℎ፜ is affected by truncation (both data type conversion
models) and round-off error (especially for the corrective terms model, because it includes numerical
derivatives). As stated in [ 20], round-off error is a major concern in the study of numerical differentia-
tion but it is not usually a problem in numerical integration. In this study, in the tests of the corrective
terms model for which the numerical differentiation settings are optimally set (as explained in Sec-
tion 3.2) it is considered that both sorts of error (truncation and round-off) are of similar magnitude.
However , for tests of the corrective terms model based on scenarios different from the one for which
the numerical differentiation settings were optimised, truncation error clearly dominates over round-off

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
the numerical differentiation settings were optimised, truncation error clearly dominates over round-off
error . These conclusions are supported by the analysis that has been carried out of the contribution
of each individual term of the summation -see Equation 4.1- to the error of the computed observable
-see an example in Figure 3.6- that shows how, after adding the last term included in the summation,
when the numerical differentiation settings are optimum (or close to it) for the scenario involved, the
error pattern of the computed observable resembles white noise; whereas when the numerical differ-
entiation settings are far from optimum for the scenario involved, a sinusoidal pattern can be clearly
observed in the error of the computed observable meaning that truncation is the dominant error .
The most important sources of truncation error present in the computed observable ℎ፜, responsible
for the error patterns observed in the conversion performance analysis of the two data type conversion
models when the error pattern is not driven by the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ or ,

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
models when the error pattern is not driven by the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ or ,
in other words, when the error level in the computation of ℎ፜ is bigger than the error level in the
computation of ℎ፜,፫, are:
• Truncation error of the numerical quadrature algorithm : this is due to the fact that when an in-
tegral is approximated numerically by applying Simpson’s or the trapezoidal method, the approx-
imation is calculated by evaluating an interpolating polynomial at a discrete set of points instead
of at an infinite amount of points along the integration interval. As the amount of points at which
the interpolating polynomial tends to infinity, the approximation tends to the exact solution. How-
ever , in practice, the amount of considered points is a choice of the user . The difference between
the approximated and the exact solution due to this phenomenon is the truncation error . This
kind of error would be present even in an ideal computer capable of storing numbers with in-
finite accuracy and not affected by round-off error [ 26]. Inequalities 3.19 and 3.20 bound the
truncation error in the approximation of the integrals given by the two implemented numerical

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
truncation error in the approximation of the integrals given by the two implemented numerical
quadrature techniques. Figure 4.9 illustrates the discussed phenomenon.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
42 4. Conversion models development
Figure 4.9: Error in the computed closed-loop Doppler observable along a complete orbit obtained from the integration-based
model with trapezoidal quadrature. Baseline simplistic analytic scenario. ጂፓ ዆ ኻኼኺ፬ ; ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . Initial time: perigee. T est: 9.
• Truncation error of the numerical differentiation stencil : this is due to the fact that the imple-
mented numerical differentiation stencils are derived truncating the T aylor series expansions that
approximate the data at the points involved in the stencil (see Equation 3.5). If it were possible
to implement in practice the T aylor series including infinite terms the obtained derivative would
not include truncation error . However , every practical calculation is done with a finite amount of
terms in the T aylor series expansion. The difference between the approximated and the exact
solution due to this phenomenon is the truncation error . This kind of error would be present
even in an ideal computer capable of storing numbers with infinite accuracy and not affected
by round-off error . Figures 4.10 and 4.11 are examples to illustrate this phenomenon. Each of

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
by round-off error . Figures 4.10 and 4.11 are examples to illustrate this phenomenon. Each of
the figures shows an error pattern due to truncation error in the computation of the numerical
derivatives. However , the two patterns are clearly different. The first one, Figure 4.10, shows a
localised region, corresponding to the perigee, with clearly bigger (truncation) error that the rest
of the orbit. That is caused by the eccentricity of the orbit, that in that test case is 𝑒 = 0.4 . The
reason behind this problem is explained in Chapter 5. On the other hand, the error pattern shown
in Figure 4.11 consists of a sinusoidal wave spanning the whole orbital period. This is due to a
misalignment of the numerical differentiation settings with respect to their optimum configuration
when the orbital velocity is faster (or the semi-major axis is smaller) than the one for which the
numerical differentiation settings were optimally tuned (the tuning process is explained in Section
3.2). The reason behind this problem is discussed in Chapter 5.

metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

simulated in Tudat. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . ፞ ዆ ኺ.ኾ , ። ዆ ኺ ∘, ፑፀፀፍ ዆ ኾ኿ ∘. Initial time: apogee. T est case: 686.

metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
5
Conversion results and discussion
After having introduced the two data type conversion mathematical models in Section 3.1, the analysis
strategy to assess their conversion performance in Section 3.2 and their software implementation
process and validation in Chapter 4, all the test cases (a summary table can be consulted in Appendix
A) have been executed in Matlab and the results have been post-processed to gain a deep insight
into the impact of every individual parameter considered in the sensitivity analysis on the conversion
performance of each of the two investigated models. The obtained results together with a discussion
of the reasons behind the observed behaviours and trends are presented in sections 5.1 and 5.2.
Additionally, a comparison of the conversion performances achieved by the two considered data type
conversion models against each other for analogous test cases is included in section 5.3. Note that
for analogous test cases, aiming at a consistent comparison between the conversion capabilities of the
studied models, both conversion algorithms have been given as input identical data sets.
5.1. Sensitivity to the inherent configuration parameters

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
studied models, both conversion algorithms have been given as input identical data sets.
5.1. Sensitivity to the inherent configuration parameters
According to the performance analysis strategy presented in section 3.2, for the analysis of the sensi-
tivity of the performance of the conversion models to their inherent configuration parameters, all the
selected combinations of inherent parameters of the models have been executed with a few different
combinations of geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios for completeness. Those configura-
tion settings are indicated in section 3.2. A complete table with the configuration settings of all the
tests performed for the sensitivity analysis of the performance of the data type conversion models to
their inherent configuration parameters can be consulted in Appendix A.1.
5.1.1. Integration-based model
The inherent parameters of this model that have been submitted to a sensitivity analysis are:
• 𝑡፨: time step between consecutive input open-loop Doppler observations. This parameter is not
strictly an inherent parameter of the model, nor it is a geometric feature of the scenarios. How-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
strictly an inherent parameter of the model, nor it is a geometric feature of the scenarios. How-
ever , since (according to the conversion results presented all along this chapter) this parameter
mostly impacts the numerical integration and differentiation that are key features of the models,
it has been decided to include it in this group. Consequently, it has been considered as an extra
inherent parameter of the models when designing and executing the tests.
• Numerical quadrature technique : mathematical expression/approximation to compute numeri-
cally the integral in equation 3.1 of a discrete input signal over a given time-span. The two
quadrature techniques considered in this sensitivity analysis, trapezoidal and Simpson’s, are pre-
sented in section 3.1.4.
• ΔT: time step between two consecutive conversions of input open-loop Doppler data into equiva-
lent closed-loop Doppler observations. It represents the time-span over which the input open-loop
Doppler observable is integrated according to equation 3.1 for the computation of the equivalent
closed-loop Doppler data.
45

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
46 5. Conversion results and discussion
Sensitivity to 𝑡፨
After thorough analysis of the results of the corresponding test cases, it is concluded that when applying
Simpson’s quadrature, the performances achieved by the integration-based model (both maximum error
along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with the target levels of accuracy) are insensitive
to variations in this parameter (for the range of tested values: 1, 2 and 5 seconds), whereas the
performances achieved by the model when applying trapezoidal integration worsen significantly as 𝑡፨
is increased, as shown in Figures 5.1 and 5.2. This is easily comprehensible from the explanation of
how the two tested integration techniques work, presented in section 3.1.4, and the explanation of
how the quality of the data type conversion is assessed, explained in Section 4.2.
For a given integration time-span, it has been observed that trapezoidal quadrature provides better
approximations to the integral the more data points available in the integration window (the smaller
the 𝑡፨). This conclusion is supported by the inequality 3.19 that bounds the truncation error of the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
the 𝑡፨). This conclusion is supported by the inequality 3.19 that bounds the truncation error of the
trapezoidal rule. On the other hand, as commented before, just based on the results of the performance
sensitivity analysis of the integration based model to variations in 𝑡፨, it seems that when applying
Simpson’s quadrature this model is insensitive to variations in this parameter . However , careful analysis
of the inequality 3.20 that bounds the error of the Simpson’s rule leads to the conclusion that the
approximations computed by applying this technique are better the smaller the 𝑡፨. The reason why this
behaviour has not been observed in the performance sensitivity analysis of this model to variations of
𝑡፨ is that for the three tested values ( 𝑡፨ = 1, 2, 5𝑠) the obtained error level in the computed observable
ℎ፜ is driven by the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, overriding the error present in the
computed observable itself (an explanation of how the quality of the computed observable is assessed
is included in Section 4.2). Figures 5.1 and 5.2 are examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
is included in Section 4.2). Figures 5.1 and 5.2 are examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena.
Note that in order to apply Simpson’s quadrature, there must be at least three different data points
distributed along the integration time-span so that the integration algorithm is able to generate the
second-order polynomial which with to approximate the signal over that region (necessary condition:
Δ𝑇 ≥ 2 · 𝑡 ፨).
As a final note, it is thought that the distribution of the input data points along the integration time-
span might potentially affect the quality of the approximation given by both integration techniques as
well. However , for the whole analysis discussed in this report, uniformly spaced data points have been
input to the two tested integration techniques.
Figure 5.1: Performance figures of the integration-based model. Baseline simplistic analytic scenario. ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T=Trapezoidal,
S=Simpson’s quadrature. T est cases: 1 to 10.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
5.1. Sensitivity to the inherent configuration parameters 47
Figure 5.2: Performance figures of the integration-based model. Baseline simplistic analytic scenario. ፭ᑠ ዆ ኼ፬ . T=Trapezoidal,
S=Simpson’s quadrature. T est cases: 11 to 20.
Sensitivity to the numerical quadrature technique
The main conclusions with regard to this configuration parameter is that the quadrature technique
has a crucial impact on the performance levels achieved by the conversion model; and that Simpson’s
numerical quadrature achieves significantly better data type conversion performances (both maximum
error along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with the target levels of accuracy) and has
a much more robust behaviour than trapezoidal quadrature along the whole set of executed tests. In
addition, some other specific, interesting features that have been observed are:
• As commented in the previous point, the performances achieved with trapezoidal quadrature are
very dependant on 𝑡፨, whereas Simpson’s ones are not affected by this parameter for the range
of tested values (1, 2 and 5s). This is due to the fact that the level of error in the computed

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of tested values (1, 2 and 5s). This is due to the fact that the level of error in the computed
observable ℎ፜ when applying Simpson’s quadrature is always smaller for the three tested values
(𝑡፨ = 1, 2, 5𝑠 ) than the error level in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, overriding the error trend in the
computed observable itself (see Figures 5.1 and 5.2).
• The performances achieved with trapezoidal quadrature are very sensitive to changes in the
geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios (specially semi-major axis and eccentricity),
whereas Simpson’s performances are not affected (or very slightly) by variations in any of the
analysed geometric parameters (a, e, i, RAAN) as discussed later in Section 5.2, being Δ𝐷 the
only exception, explained in the next bullet point. Further discussion of the influence of geometric
features of the simulated scenarios on the performances achieved by the integration-based model
when applying Simpson’s quadrature is included in section 5.2.1.
• The conversion performances achieved with Simpson’s quadrature are limited in all the executed
test cases by the finite accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫. This was suspected because of

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
test cases by the finite accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫. This was suspected because of
the random nature of the error along a complete orbit, and confirmed by testing that when the
interplanetary distance Δ𝐷 between the Earth and Mars is reduced (analytic simplistic scenarios),
the accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ increases and consequently the performances achieved by
Simpson’s quadrature improve by the same factor by which the Earth-Mars distance is reduced.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
48 5. Conversion results and discussion
Further discussion of the influence of this geometric feature on the performances achieved by the
integration-based model is included in section 5.2.1. Examples that illustrate this phenomenon
are Figure 5.13. The data included in T able 4.1 supports the discussed phenomena as well.
• Trapezoidal quadrature achieves its best performances of the whole set of executed tests for
larger orbits (slower orbital velocity), getting to performances very similar to those achieved by
Simpson’s quadrature (limited by the accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫). Figure 5.10 is an
example that illustrates this phenomenon.
The fact of finding a quadrature method (Simpson’s) that performs (sometimes) equal or (in most
of the cases) significantly better than the other one (trapezoidal) has driven the decision of not in-
cluding a deep discussion of the latter in the performance sensitivity analysis of the integration based
conversion model to the geometric features of the simulated scenarios included in section 5.2.1 (just
a few examples will be shown to support the previous discussion).

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
a few examples will be shown to support the previous discussion).
Finally, it is interesting to note that the conclusion that Simpson’s quadrature generally achieves
better performances that the trapezoidal rule is in concordance with the comparison of the inequalities
that bound the truncation error of both numerical integration techniques given by Equations 3.19 and
3.20.
Sensitivity to ΔT
In general (for all the tested scenario geometric settings and 𝑡፨), it is concluded that the maximum
error of the integration-based model along a complete orbit improves (decreases) as ΔT increases,
regardless of the numerical quadrature technique applied, as shown in Figures 5.1 and 5.2.
However , the explanation for this behaviour is different for each of the two numerical quadrature
techniques applied. On the one hand, when applying trapezoidal quadrature, this trend is explained
because of the reduction of the error in the numerical computation of the integral in Equation 2.7 thanks
to its division into Δ𝑇. It can be observed in Figures 5.1 and 5.2, for example, that even though Simp-
son’s quadrature provides in all cases lower levels of error than the trapezoidal rule (Simpson’s rule has

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
son’s quadrature provides in all cases lower levels of error than the trapezoidal rule (Simpson’s rule has
smaller truncation error than trapezoidal, according to inequalities 3.19 and 3.20), for both quadrature
techniques the maximum error in the computed observable along a complete orbit decreases as Δ𝑇 in
increased. On the other hand, when applying Simpson’s quadrature, this trend is explained because of
the reduction of the error in the computation of ℎ፜,፫ as Δ𝑇 is increased, that can be directly concluded
from the expression for the computation of ℎ፜,፫ given by Equation 4.2. That reduction of the maximum
error along a complete orbit is, in agreement with the aforementioned equation, by a factor of ኻ
ጂፓ .
For a better understanding of the error present in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, the discussion included in
Section 4.2 might be useful.
In some cases, it has been observed that when applying the trapezoidal quadrature technique, there
is a point from which further increases of ΔT do not lead to further improvements in the maximum error
of the computed observable along a complete orbit. This phenomenon can be observed, for example,

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of the computed observable along a complete orbit. This phenomenon can be observed, for example,
in Figure 5.1 (for Δ𝑇 values of 90 and 120s) and also in Figure 5.3 (for all the tested Δ𝑇 values).
It is believed that this behaviour is caused by truncation error of the algorithm for the trapezoidal
integration since Simpson’s quadrature leads to significantly better conversion performances for its
counterpart tests.
Regarding the percentages of compliance with the target levels of accuracy, on the one hand it is
concluded that, when applying Simpson’s integration they improve as ΔT is increased. This coincides
with the expectation since the errors obtained with Simpson’s technique (random noise) decrease by
a factor of ኻ
ጂፓ (the errors are driven by the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, as explained
in Section 4.2) as ΔT increases, whereas the target accuracy levels decrease by a factor of ኻ
√ጂፓ as
ΔT increases. This means that the decrease in maximum error of the observable along a complete
orbit is faster or stronger than the reduction of the target accuracy levels as a function of ΔT (given

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
orbit is faster or stronger than the reduction of the target accuracy levels as a function of ΔT (given
by Eq. 3.23). On the other hand, when applying trapezoidal integration, there is not a clear trend
in the evolution of the percentages of compliance as a function of ΔT , being the observed behaviour
also a function of 𝑡፨. In the majority of the cases it has been observed that the percentages of
compliance decrease as ΔT increases. This fact has not been further investigated since, as explained
in the previous point, the performances achieved when applying trapezoidal integration are generally
clearly worse than those achieved by Simpson’s, proving that the trapezoidal quadrature rule is not
suitable for this open-loop Doppler data conversion approach.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
5.1. Sensitivity to the inherent configuration parameters 49
Figure 5.3: Performance figures of the integration-based model. T=Trapezoidal, S=Simpson’s quadrature. Realistic scenario
simulated in Tudat. Geometric settings: ፞ ዆ ኺ.ኾ , ። ዆ ኾ኿ ∘ and ፑፀፀፍ ዆ ኺ ∘. ፭ᑠ ዆ ኿፬ . T est cases: 261 to 270.
5.1.2. Corrective terms model
For this data type conversion model, the inherent parameters of the model that have been submitted
to a performance sensitivity analysis are:
• 𝑡፨: time-step between consecutive input open-loop Doppler observations. This parameter is
not strictly an inherent parameter of the model, nor it is a geometric feature of the scenarios.
However , since (according to the results) this parameter mostly impacts the numerical integration
and differentiation that are key aspects of the models themselves, it has been decided to include
it in this group. Consequently, it has been considered as an extra inherent parameter of the
models when designing and executing the tests.
• ΔT: time step between two consecutive conversions of input data into equivalent closed-loop
Doppler observations. It represents the time-span along which the input open-loop Doppler

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
Doppler observations. It represents the time-span along which the input open-loop Doppler
observable is integrated according to Equation 3.1 for the computation of the equivalent closed-
loop Doppler observations.
It is worth recalling at this point that the corrective terms model, for the simplistic analytic scenarios
(those for which there is an analytic expression for the derivatives of the range) has been implemented
in two different ways: the actual one, where the derivatives of the range (from the second to the sixth)
are computed numerically (N), plus an extra one in which all the derivatives are computed analytically
(A). This has served as validation for the model and the numerical differentiation settings by allowing
a direct comparison of the results achieved when applying numerical differentiation (with its inherent
associated errors) against those achieved when computing the derivatives analytically (not affected by
truncation error). It has also been helpful to understand which are the highest performances achievable
by the model when working with double precision variables and floating-point arithmetic.

text:
50 5. Conversion results and discussion
Sensitivity to 𝑡፨
This model has shown to be completely insensitive to variations in 𝑡፨ for the range of tested values (1,
2 and 5s), regardless of the geometric configuration of the simulated scenarios.
This can be explained due to the fact that all the configured time-steps ( 𝛿𝑡፧ ) involved in the numer-
ical derivatives of the range (a different time-step has been set for each derivative order) are divisible
by all the tested values for 𝑡፨ giving zero as reminder . This means that for every derivative order (from
the second to the sixth), at every time where ℎ፜ was to be computed, there has always been informa-
tion at all the required neighbouring time points to compute the numerical derivatives optimally (recall
that the input data sets consist of discrete observations of range and open-loop Doppler at certain
equispaced times). On the contrary, if for the computation of the numerical derivatives of the range
at any neighbouring point around specific time at which ℎ፜ was to be computed there had not been
observations (in the input data set) at the times required by the numerical differentiation stencil some-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
observations (in the input data set) at the times required by the numerical differentiation stencil some-
thing must have been done, like taking the closest observation available (this is the option that I have
implemented) or interpolating, hence corroding the conversion performance of the model. Anyway, it
would not be strictly due to the 𝑡፨ parameter itself, but because of the lack of input observations at
the required points for the computation of the numerical derivatives of the range with the configured
numerical differentiation settings (stencil and 𝛿𝑡፧ ).
In principle, regardless of setting a smaller or bigger 𝑡፨, the conversion performance of the model
should not be affected provided that the input data set has data at the times required by the imple-
mented numerical differentiation stencil; or in other words, provided that the configured 𝛿𝑡፧ for each
numerical derivative order are such that there are range/Doppler observations in the input data set at
the required times around any time at which ℎ፜ is to be computed.
Sensitivity to ΔT
In most of the executed test cases, it is observed that the maximum error along an orbit of the computed

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
Sensitivity to ΔT
In most of the executed test cases, it is observed that the maximum error along an orbit of the computed
observable obtained by application of the corrective terms conversion model improves (decreases) as
ΔT increases, regardless of the 𝑡፨. This is explained because of the reduction of the error in the
computation of ℎ፜,፫ as ΔT is increased, that can be directly concluded from the expression for the
computation of ℎ፜,፫ presented in Equation 4.2. That reduction of the maximum error along a complete
orbit is, in agreement with the aforementioned equation, by a factor of ኻ
ጂፓ , leading to the conclusion
that the errors are driven by the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫.
Regarding the percentages of compliance with the target levels of accuracy, it has been observed
that in most of the executed test cases they improve as ΔT is increased. This coincides with the
expectation since, as commented before, the maximum error along an orbit of the computed observable
decreases by a factor of ኻ
ጂፓ (driven by the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫), whereas the
target accuracy levels decrease by a factor of ኻ

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
ጂፓ (driven by the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫), whereas the
target accuracy levels decrease by a factor of ኻ
√ጂፓ . This means that the decrease in maximum error of
the observable along a complete orbit is faster or stronger than the reduction of the target accuracy
levels as a function of ΔT (given by Eq. 3.23). For a better understanding of the nature of the error
present in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, the discussion included in Section 4.2 might be useful.
In the majority of the test cases based on analytic scenarios, it has also been observed that the
conversion performances achieved by the model (when computing the derivatives numerically - N in
the figures) are very similar to the highest performances achievable by the algorithm (in absence of
truncation error), indicated by the results of its implementation computing the derivatives of the range
analytically (A in the figures).
There are, however , two clear exceptions to the commonly observed behaviour:
• Variations in the semi-major axis (simplistic analytic scenarios): if the semi-major axis is reduced
with respect to its value for which the numerical differentiation settings were optimised (see

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
with respect to its value for which the numerical differentiation settings were optimised (see
explanation in Section 3.2), the circular orbital velocity of the orbit becomes too fast for the con-
figured numerical differentiation settings, consequently increasing the error in the computation
of the numerical derivatives of the range and the maximum error in the computation of ℎ፜ along
a complete orbit. In these cases, the general trend is inverted and the maximum error in the
computation of ℎ፜ along a complete orbit increases with ΔT , as shown in Figure 5.4.
• Variations in eccentricity (realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat): whenever there is eccentricity
(𝑒 ≠ 0 ), the dynamics of the orbital motion vary all along the orbit leading to faster motion

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
5.1. Sensitivity to the inherent configuration parameters 51
around the perigee and slower motion in the vicinity of the apogee compared to the circular
velocity that there would be in a circular orbit with its radius equal to the semi-major axis of
that elliptic orbit. If the eccentricity is high enough such that the velocity at the perigee is too
fast for the configured numerical differentiation settings, then the error in the computation of
the numerical derivatives increases and consequently the maximum error in the computation of
ℎ፜ along a complete orbit increases (dramatically for high e values) as well. In these cases, the
general trend is also inverted and the maximum error in the computation of ℎ፜ along a complete
orbit increases with ΔT , as shown in Figure 5.6.
The explanation for both phenomena is the same and lies on the equation for the computation of the
closed-loop Doppler observable, that is shown here below again for convenience:

Looking at the equation, and due to the fact that the numerical derivatives are identical regardless of
ΔT (depending on ΔT , there are more or less points where ℎ፜ has to be computed and consequently the
numerical derivatives, but for the ℎ፜ points that coincide for every ΔT case, the numerical derivatives
are identical regardless of the value of ΔT), the increase in maximum error along a complete orbit
with ΔT can be observed directly from the term (−Δ𝑇)።ዅኻ. The higher the ΔT , the higher the error in
every term of the summation ( ΔT acts as an amplifier of the error of the numerical derivatives that it
is multiplying), and consequently the higher the error in the computed ℎ፜.
Notwithstanding their common explanation, there is an important difference between the two dis-
cussed exceptions: the decrease of the percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets is stronger
in the first exception than in the latter . This can be explained because whenever there is eccentricity,
only in the part of the orbit close to the perigee the increase of the orbital velocity might be such that

the numerical derivatives lose quality; whilst whenever the semi-major axis is decreased, although the
new (higher) orbital velocity might be slower than the velocity at the perigee in exception one, it is
constant all along the circular orbit.
These phenomena can be observed, for example, in the following two pairs of figures, which show
the error in the computed observable ℎ፜ along a complete orbit and the conversion performance figures
of the corrective terms model as a function of ΔT for two different scenarios: one where the semi-
major axis has been reduced with respect to its value for which the numerical differentiation settings
were optimised (figures 5.4 and 5.5) and the other one where the eccentricity has been set to a value
different from zero (figures 5.6 and 5.7).
