A surprising phenomenon observed in the conversion results obtained for the tests of the sensitivity
analysis of the performance of the corrective terms model to variations in ΔT from realistic scenarios
simulated in Tudat is that for the highest ΔT (90, 120s) among the range of tested values, the max-
imum error along a complete orbit does not decrease any more (or even slightly worsens), and the
percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets start decreasing gently, as shown in figure 5.8.

text:
54 5. Conversion results and discussion
It is believed that the reason behind this effect is the error in the lower order derivatives (specially the
second) of the range included in the calculation of ℎ፜ by application of the corrective terms model. From
the analysis the equation for the computation of ℎ፜, that is shown here below again for convenience, and
knowing that the level of error in the numerical derivatives of the range (for the configured numerical
differentiation settings) decrease by around three orders of magnitude for every successively higher
order derivative (as shown in Figure 5.9), it is concluded that for high ΔT values, the error in the lowest
order derivative of the range (the second derivative, that is the one with the highest level of error), that
is multiplied by ΔT (raised to the power of one), might surpass the accuracy level in the computation
of ℎ፜,፫ and, consequently, deteriorate the performance of the model.

text:
5.2. Sensitivity to the geometry of the simulated scenarios 55
5.2. Sensitivity to the geometry of the simulated scenarios
According to the performance analysis strategy presented in section 3.2, for the analysis of the sensitiv-
ity of the performance of the data type conversion mathematical models to the geometric parameters
of the simulated scenarios, all the selected geometric parameters combinations (different scenario ge-
ometries) have been executed with a few different combinations of inherent parameters of the models
for completeness. Those configuration settings are indicated in section 3.2. A complete table with
the configuration settings of all the tests performed for the sensitivity analysis of the performance of
the mathematical models to the geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios can be consulted in
Appendix A.2.
For both approaches, the geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios that have been submitted
to a performance sensitivity analysis are exactly the same:
T ested with simplistic analytic scenarios:
• Semi-major axis ( a): the major axis of an ellipse is the longest straight line possible between two

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
T ested with simplistic analytic scenarios:
• Semi-major axis ( a): the major axis of an ellipse is the longest straight line possible between two
points of the ellipse. It passes through the two foci and the centre. The semi-major axis is a
half of it. It spans from the centre of the ellipse, passing through a focus all straight until the
perimeter of the ellipse.
• Orbital plane orientation angle in simplistic analytic scenarios ( 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ): is the angle between the
orbital plane and the imaginary line connecting the (fixed) ground station to the centre of the
orbit. In regular orbital elements, it represents the inclination ( 𝑖).
• Interplanetary distance ( Δ𝐷): in the simplistic analytic scenarios, it is the distance between the
(fixed) ground station (Earth) and the centre of the central body of the orbiter (Mars).
T ested with realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat:
• Eccentricity ( e): this parameters expresses the ratio between the major an minor axes of the
ellipse. It gives an idea of how close to a circle is the shape of the ellipse.
• Inclination ( 𝑖): traditional orbital element indicating the vertical tilt of the orbital plane with re-
spect to a reference plane.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
• Inclination ( 𝑖): traditional orbital element indicating the vertical tilt of the orbital plane with re-
spect to a reference plane.
• Right Ascension of the Ascending Node ( RAAN): traditional orbital element that represents the
angle from a reference direction (origin of longitudes) to the direction of the ascending node,
measured along the reference plane.
5.2.1. Integration-based model
In this section the individual sensitivity analysis of the performance of the integration-based model to
each of the considered geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios are presented.
Sensitivity to semi-major axis ( a)
The conversion performance of this model is insensitive (it shows tiny changes) to variations in a when
applying Simpson’s quadrature technique (S). When applying trapezoidal quadrature (T), the higher
the value of a relative to its baseline value ( 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚), the better the conversion performance
of the model (both in terms of maximum error in a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with
the accuracy targets). More specifically, when applying trapezoidal quadrature this model achieves

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
the accuracy targets). More specifically, when applying trapezoidal quadrature this model achieves
its best observed performances along the whole set of executed tests for values of the semi-major
axis bigger than its baseline value A ( 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚), achieving performances similar to those
reached by applying Simpson’s quadrature (limited by the finite accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫).
This dependency is very strong and can be explained due to the low-order (first order) interpolating
polynomial applied in the trapezoidal rule compared to the second other interpolating polynomial applied
in Simpson’s rule (refer to section 3.1.4). For faster orbits, the change in the observable in a time
interval 𝑡፨ is bigger than for slower orbits (orbits with bigger a) which means that approximating the
integral in time of the observable along that interval by the average (see Equation 3.17) of its more
different extreme values is a worse approximation than it would be if both extreme values were more
similar (for slower orbits). This leads to an improvement in the accuracy of the approximation of the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
56 5. Conversion results and discussion
integral over time of the input open-loop Doppler observable (see equation 3.1) for higher a values
and, consequently, to an improvement in the conversion performance of the model.
Figure 5.10 can serve as an example to illustrate the discussed phenomena.
Figure 5.10: Performance figures of the integration-based conversion model. Sensitivity to ፚ. T=Trapezoidal, S=Simpson’s
quadrature. Simplistic analytic scenarios with ጂፃ ዆ ፃ and ።ᑒᑟᑒᑝ ዆ ኺ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T est cases: 406, 409, 412, 433, 436
and 439.
Sensitivity to orbital plane orientation angle in simplistic analytic scenarios ( 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ )
Increasing the angle between the orbital plane and the vector connecting the ground station (GS) and
the centre of the orbit has a tiny impact on the conversion performance (both in terms of maximum
error in a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets) of the model when
applying Simpson’s quadrature technique (S). The effect (tiny) is such that the higher the inclination
angle, the worse the performance of the model. On the contrary, increasing the angle improves the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
angle, the worse the performance of the model. On the contrary, increasing the angle improves the
conversion performance of the model (both in terms of maximum error in a complete orbit and per-
centages of compliance with the accuracy targets) when applying trapezoidal quadrature (T).
Analogously to the explanation given in the previous point, this is due to the low-order (first order)
interpolating polynomial used by the trapezoidal quadrature algorithm compared to the second other
interpolating polynomial used by Simpson’s quadrature (refer to section 3.1.4). The effect of increasing
the 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ angle is reducing the magnitude of the velocity of the orbiter with respect to the ground station
along the line of sight (LOS) as shown in figure 5.11, which is equivalent to increasing a in the sense
that it reduces the magnitude of the range rate (Doppler observable) evolution in time. According
to that, for faster LOS velocities (smaller 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ), the change in the observable in a time interval 𝑡፨ is
bigger than for slower LOS velocities (orbits with bigger 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ) which means that approximating the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
bigger than for slower LOS velocities (orbits with bigger 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ) which means that approximating the
integral in time of the observable along that interval by the average of its more different extreme values
is a worse approximation than it would be if both extreme values were more similar (for slower LOS
velocities, achieved in orbits with bigger 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ). This leads to an improvement in the accuracy of the
approximation of the integral in time of the input Doppler observable (see equation 3.1) for higher
𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ values and, consequently, to an improvement in the conversion performance of the model.
In general, Simpson’s quadrature performances are clearly better than those achieved by applying
the trapezoidal quadrature technique.
Figures 5.11 and 5.12 can serve as examples to illustrate the discussed phenomena.

text:
58 5. Conversion results and discussion
Sensitivity to interplanetary distance ( Δ𝐷)
In general, both applying trapezoidal (T) or Simpson’s quadrature (S), it is observed that reducing
Δ𝐷 with respect to its baseline value ( 𝐷 = 2.25 · 10 ኻኻ𝑚) improves the conversion performance (both
maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets) of
the model for every combination of the other geometric parameters ( a, 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ). In the case of Simpson’s
quadrature, this behaviour is explained due to the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, accuracy
that increases as the interplanetary distance is reduced, according to the explanation given in section
4.2. However , the reason behind this trend in the case of applying the trapezoidal rule is not obvious
and it has not been further investigated since, as explained in the previous point, the performances
achieved when applying trapezoidal integration are generally clearly worse than those achieved by
Simpson’s, proving that the trapezoidal quadrature rule is not suitable for this open-loop Doppler data

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
Simpson’s, proving that the trapezoidal quadrature rule is not suitable for this open-loop Doppler data
conversion approach. For the particular case of the performance sensitivity analysis to Δ𝐷, it has been
checked that the conversion performances (both) achieved by applying Simpson’s quadrature are in all
the executed tests equal or better than those obtained by applying trapezoidal quadrature.
Figures 5.13 and 5.14 show examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena.
Figure 5.13: Performance figures of the integration-based conversion model. Sensitivity to ጂፃ . T=Trapezoidal, S=Simpson’s
quadrature. Simplistic analytic scenarios with ፚ ዆ ፀ and ።ᑒᑟᑒᑝ ዆ ኺ ∘. ጂ T=60s, ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T ests: 406, 415, 424, 433, 442 and 451.
Figure 5.14: Performance figures of the integration-based model. Sensitivity to ጂፃ . T=Trapezoidal, S=Simpson’s quadrature.
Simplistic analytic scenarios with ፚ ዆ ኽፀ and ።ᑒᑟᑒᑝ ዆ ዀኺ ∘. ጂ T=60s, ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T ests: 414, 423, 432, 441, 450 and 459.

text:
5.2. Sensitivity to the geometry of the simulated scenarios 59
A final interesting note about the parameter Δ𝐷, even though it is not of much practical interest
for the analysis of real missions, is that it has been observed that if Δ𝐷 is set to a value such that the
orbiter passes close to the ground station (GS), the performances of the model significantly degrade
at the surroundings of the point of closest approach to the GS. This has been observed in special tests
run with the parameter Δ𝐷 set to ፃ
ኻኺᎶ . The reason behind this local bad performance is the sudden
change from maximum range-rate of the orbiter approaching the GS to maximum range-rate going
away from it. It happens due to the fact that, being the orbit circular , the maximum relative velocities
(approaching and going away) of the orbiter with respect to the GS along the line of sight (LOS) take
place when the LOS is tangent to the orbit. As Δ𝐷 is reduced, these two points become closer and
closer (coincident at some point, depending on the magnitude of the semi-major axis) making the
dynamics of the motion of the orbiter at the surroundings of the point of closest approach so fast that

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
dynamics of the motion of the orbiter at the surroundings of the point of closest approach so fast that
approximating the integral in time of the input Doppler observable along a 𝑡፨ interval by the average
of its very different extreme values is a worse approximation than it would be if both extreme values
were more similar (for slower changes in the Doppler observable, achieved with geometries in which
the orbiter does not pass so close to the GS).
Sensitivity to eccentricity ( e)
The performance of the model (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of compli-
ance with the accuracy targets) is insensitive (it shows negligible changes) to variations in eccentricity
when applying Simpson’s quadrature (S).
When applying trapezoidal quadrature (T), the maximum error in a complete orbit increases dramat-
ically with the eccentricity. This dependency is very strong and can be explained due to the low-order
(first order) interpolating polynomial used by the trapezoidal quadrature algorithm (refer to section
3.1.4). Whenever there is eccentricity, the dynamics of the orbit become much faster close to the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
3.1.4). Whenever there is eccentricity, the dynamics of the orbit become much faster close to the
perigee than in the nominal circular orbit and hence, the change of the input Doppler observable in
a time interval 𝑡፨ in that region is significantly bigger than in the rest of the orbit, which means that
approximating close to the perigee the integral in time of the open-loop Doppler observable along the
time interval 𝑡፨ by the average of its more different extreme values is a worse approximation than it
would be if both extreme values where more similar (far from the perigee, or without eccentricity. This
leads to a deterioration in the accuracy of the approximation of the integral in time of the input Doppler
observable (see equation 3.1) for e values different from zero and, consequently, to worse conversion
performances of the model.
Regarding the percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets, it is observed that they improve
as e is increased. This is related to the fact that the degradation of the performance of the model due
to e is a local phenomenon that takes place around the perigee. Along the rest of the orbit, the orbital

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
to e is a local phenomenon that takes place around the perigee. Along the rest of the orbit, the orbital
velocity is reduced with respect to the nominal orbit due to the eccentricity, leading to an improved
performance of the model in that region relative to the baseline scenario and, hence, increasing the
percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets.
Figures 5.15 and 5.16 show examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena. Note in Figure 5.16
how increasing the eccentricity (plot on the right) leads, simultaneously, to clearly worse error in the
computed observable along the vicinity of the perigee and to significantly better error levels along
the rest of the orbit compared to the case of the circular orbit (plot on the left). That is the reason
why, surprisingly, even though the maximum error in the computed observable along a complete orbit
worsens dramatically as 𝑒 increases, the percentages of compliance with the target accuracy levels
follow the opposite trend as shown in Figure 5.15.

metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:

Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat with ።, ፑፀፀፍ ዆ ኺ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T ests:

፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . Initial time: apogee. T est cases: 517 and 526, respectively.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package

metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
5.2. Sensitivity to the geometry of the simulated scenarios 61
Sensitivity to inclination angle ( 𝑖)
The performance of the data type conversion model (both maximum error along a complete orbit and
percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets) is insensitive to variations in inclination angle
(i) when applying Simpson’s quadrature technique (S). When applying trapezoidal quadrature (T),
it is observed that in most of the cases, the higher the inclination angle, the better the conversion
performance of the model (both in terms of maximum error in a complete orbit and percentages of
compliance with the accuracy targets).
Analogously to the explanation for the orientation angle of the orbital plane in the simplistic ana-
lytic scenarios ( 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ), the observed trend is explained due to the low-order (first order) interpolating
polynomial used by the trapezoidal quadrature algorithm compared to the second other interpolating
polynomial used by Simpson’s quadrature (refer to section 3.1.4). Exactly as already commented for
the case of 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ , the effect of increasing i is reducing the magnitude of the velocity of the orbiter with

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
the case of 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ , the effect of increasing i is reducing the magnitude of the velocity of the orbiter with
respect to the ground station along the line of sight (LOS) ans shown in Figure 5.11, which is equiv-
alent to increasing a in the sense that it reduces the magnitude of the range rate (open-loop Doppler
observable) evolution in time. According to that, for faster LOS velocities (smaller i), the change in
the observable in a time interval 𝑡፨ is bigger than for slower LOS velocities (orbits with bigger i) which
means that approximating the integral in time of the observable along that interval by the average of its
more different extreme values is a worse approximation than it would be if both extreme values were
more similar (for slower LOS velocities, achieved in orbits with bigger i). This leads to an improvement
in the accuracy of the approximation of the integral over time of the input open-loop Doppler observ-
able (see equation 3.1) for higher i values and, consequently, to an improvement in the performance
of the model.
In general, for the performance sensitivity analysis of the model to variations in i, Simpson’s quadra-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of the model.
In general, for the performance sensitivity analysis of the model to variations in i, Simpson’s quadra-
ture conversion performances are clearly better than those achieved by applying the trapezoidal quadra-
ture technique.
Figure 5.17 shows an example that illustrates the discussed phenomena.
Figure 5.17: Performance figures of the integration-based model. Sensitivity to ።. T=Trapezoidal, S=Simpson’s quadrature.
Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat with ፞ ዆ ኺ.ኾ , ፑፀፀፍ ዆ ኾ኿ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T ests: 524, 527, 530, 551, 554 and 557.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
62 5. Conversion results and discussion
Sensitivity to Right Ascension of the Ascending Node ( RAAN )
The performances (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with
accuracy targets) of the model show a weak dependency on this parameter when applying Simpson’s
quadrature technique (S). In general, Simpson’s quadrature performances (both) are clearly better
than those achieved by applying the trapezoidal rule.
When applying trapezoidal quadrature (T), there is no general trend describing the conversion
performances of this model as a function of RAAN. The model performs differently depending on the
configuration of the other geometric parameters: e and i. However , this relation has not been further
investigated since, as already commented in the previous points, the performances achieved when
applying trapezoidal integration are generally clearly worse than those achieved by Simpson’s, proving
that the trapezoidal quadrature rule is not suitable for this open-loop Doppler data conversion approach.
Figures 5.18 and 5.19 show examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena.

metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
Figures 5.18 and 5.19 show examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena.

text:
5.2. Sensitivity to the geometry of the simulated scenarios 63
5.2.2. Corrective terms model
In this section the individual sensitivity analysis of the performance of the corrective terms data type
conversion model to each of the considered geometric parameters of the simulated scenarios are
presented.
Sensitivity to semi-major axis ( a)
In general (regardless of the 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ , Δ𝐷 and inherent parameters of the model), the conversion per-
formances of this model are very high (maximum error in the computed observable along a complete
orbit smaller than the most demanding accuracy target and around 100% compliance with the three
target accuracy levels) for a settings equal or bigger than its baseline value A ( 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚). On
the contrary, the performances are significantly degraded as a is given smaller values than its baseline.
It has been observed that for a settings equal or bigger than its baseline value 𝐴, the conversion
performances achieved by this model (calculating the derivatives numerically - N in the figures) are
similar to the highest possible achievable performances, indicated by the implementation of the model

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
similar to the highest possible achievable performances, indicated by the implementation of the model
with all the derivatives computed analytically (A in the figures). On the contrary, when a is set to lower
values than its baseline value (corresponding to higher circular orbital velocities), the performances
worsen significantly with respect to the highest possible achievable performances. This trend is ex-
plained due to the faster dynamics of the orbit when the semi-major axis is reduced with respect to the
value for which the numerical differentiation settings where optimised (according to the explanation
given in Section 3.2), that can not be accurately captured by the configured numerical differentiation
settings.
It is interesting to note that the decrease in performance due to a reduction of the semi-major
axis with respect to the value for which the numerical differentiation settings where optimised is a
phenomenon that degrades the performance of the model along the complete orbit, unlike the case of
the eccentricity ( 𝑒) that degrades the performance of the orbit locally, in the vicinity of the perigee as
explained later in this section (see Figure 5.27).

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
explained later in this section (see Figure 5.27).
Figures 5.20 and 5.21 show examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena.
Figure 5.20: Error in the computed observable ( ፡ᑔ,ᑣ ዅ ፡ ᑔ) along a complete orbit obtained by application of the corrective terms
model. Simplistic analytic scenario with ፚ ዆ ኺ.኿ፀ , ።ᑒᑟᑒᑝ ዆ ኺ ∘ and ጂፃ ዆ ፃ . ጂ T዆ ኻኼኺ፬ . T est case: 652.

64 5. Conversion results and discussion
Figure 5.21: Performance figures of the corrective terms conversion model. Sensitivity to ፚ. N=Numerical, A=Analytic derivatives
of the range. Simplistic analytic scenarios with ጂፃ ዆ ᐻ
ᎳᎲᎲ and ።ᑒᑟᑒᑝ ዆ ዀኺ ∘. ጂ T=120s, ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T est cases: 660, 663 and 666.
Sensitivity to the orientation angle of the orbital plane in simplistic analytic scenarios ( 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ )
The conversion performances (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of com-
pliance with accuracy targets) of this model show a weak dependency on this parameter when the
semi-major axis ( a) takes values equal or bigger than its baseline (numerical differentiation settings
capable of capturing the dynamics of the orbit) as shown in Figure 5.22. In the cases where a is set to
smaller values than its baseline (higher circular orbital velocity resulting in the numerical differentiation
scheme not being able of properly capturing properly the dynamics of the orbit), it has been observed
that the conversion performances (both) of the model improve as 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ is increased, as shown in Figure
5.23.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
that the conversion performances (both) of the model improve as 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ is increased, as shown in Figure
5.23.
This is explained because the effect of increasing 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ is reducing the magnitude of the velocity of
the orbiter with respect to the ground station along the line of sight (see figure 5.11), which is equivalent
to increasing a in the sense that it reduces the magnitude of the range rate (Doppler observable)
evolution in time. This leads to an improvement in the performance of the numerical differentiation
scheme and consequently to an improvement in the performance of the computed observable.
It has also been observed that for the cases with a equal or bigger than its baseline value 𝐴, the
conversion performances achieved by the model (calculating the derivatives numerically - N in the
figures) are similar to the highest possible achievable performances, indicated by the implementation
of the model with all the derivatives computed analytically (A in the figures), for every 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ . On the
contrary, for the cases in which a is set to lower values than its baseline (corresponding to higher cir-

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
contrary, for the cases in which a is set to lower values than its baseline (corresponding to higher cir-
cular orbital velocities), the achieved conversion performances are significantly worse than the highest
possible achievable for every 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ due to the faster dynamics of the orbit that can not be accurately
captured by the numerical differentiation scheme.
The following figures show examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena. Figure 5.22 shows
the sensitivity of the performance of the model to variations of 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ when the semi-major axis takes
equal or bigger values than its baseline A ( 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚). Figure 5.23 shows the sensitivity of the
performance of the model to variations of 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ when the semi-major axis is set to smaller values than
its baseline A.

text:
66 5. Conversion results and discussion
Sensitivity to interplanetary distance ( Δ𝐷)
The conversion performances (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of compli-
ance with accuracy targets) of this model are better the smaller the Δ𝐷, except for the cases in which
the semi-major axis ( 𝑎) is set to smaller values than its baseline 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚 (higher circular
orbital velocity resulting in the numerical differentiation scheme not being able of properly capturing
the dynamics of the orbit).
This is explained because of the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, accuracy that increases
as the interplanetary distance Δ𝐷 is reduced, according to the explanation given in section 4.2.
In addition, it is observed that in the cases in which the semi-major axis ( a) is set to equal or
bigger values than its baseline, the performances achieved by the model (calculating the derivatives
numerically - N in the figures) are similar to the highest achievable performances, indicated by the
implementation of the model with all the derivatives computed analytically (A in the figures), for every

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
implementation of the model with all the derivatives computed analytically (A in the figures), for every
𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ . On the contrary, for the cases in which a is set to lower values than its baseline (corresponding
to higher circular orbital velocities), the performances are significantly worse than the highest possible
achievable for every 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ due to the faster dynamics of the orbit that can not be accurately captured
by the numerical differentiation scheme.
Figures 5.24 and 5.25 show examples that illustrate the discussed phenomena.
A final interesting note about the parameter Δ𝐷, even though it is not of much practical interest
for the analysis of real missions, is that if Δ𝐷 is set to a value such that the orbiter passes close to the
ground station (GS), the performances of the model significantly degrade at the surroundings of the
point of closest approach to the GS. This has been observed in special tests run with the parameter
Δ𝐷 set to ፃ
ኻኺᎶ . The reason behind this local bad performance is the sudden change from maximum
range-rate of the orbiter approaching the GS to maximum range-rate going away from it. It happens

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
range-rate of the orbiter approaching the GS to maximum range-rate going away from it. It happens
due to the fact that, being the orbit circular , the maximum relative velocities (approaching and going
away) of the orbiter with respect to the GS along the line of sight (LOS) take place when the LOS
is tangent to the orbit. As Δ𝐷 is reduced, these two points become closer and closer (coincident at
some point, depending on the magnitude of the semi-major axis) making the dynamics of the motion
of the orbiter at the surroundings of the point of closest approach too fast for the configured numerical
differentiation settings.
Figure 5.24: Performance figures of the corrective terms model. Sensitivity to ጂፃ . N=Numerical, A=Analytic derivatives of the
range. Simplistic analytic scenarios with ፚ ዆ ኽፀ and ።ᑒᑟᑒᑝ ዆ ዀኺ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T ests: 630, 639 and 648.

metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
5.2. Sensitivity to the geometry of the simulated scenarios 67
Figure 5.25: Performance figures of the corrective terms model. Sensitivity to ጂፃ . N=Numerical, A=Analytic derivatives of the
range. Simplistic analytic scenarios with ፚ ዆ ኺ.኿ፀ and ።ᑒᑟᑒᑝ ዆ ኺ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T ests: 625, 634 and 643.
Sensitivity to eccentricity ( e)
There is a clear , general trend for the performance of the model as a function of the eccentricity of the
orbit: the bigger the eccentricity of the orbit, the worse the performances of the model (both maximum
error along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets), as shown in
Figure 5.26. This dependency is very strong. The maximum error in the computed observable along a
complete orbit, in particular , worsens dramatically as 𝑒 is increased.
The reason behind the observed behaviour of the maximum error in the computed observable along
a complete orbit is that, whenever there is eccentricity ( 𝑒 ≠ 0 ), the dynamics of the orbit become much
faster close to the perigee (and much slower close to the apogee) than in the nominal circular orbit

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
faster close to the perigee (and much slower close to the apogee) than in the nominal circular orbit
for which the numerical differentiation settings were optimised (according to the explanation given in
Section 3.2), and hence the configured numerical differentiation settings are not capable of properly
capturing the dynamics of the orbital motion in that region, as shown in Figure 5.27. Regarding the
percentages of compliance with the target levels of accuracy, they are also degraded as 𝑒 is increased.
This is due to the fact that the higher the eccentricity of the orbit, the larger the region of the orbit
where the orbital velocity surpasses that for which the numerical differentiation settings were optimally
tune, and consequently the smaller is the region where the error in the computed observable is below
the target error levels.
It is interesting to note that the decrease in performance due to the eccentricity is a local phe-
nomenon (see Figure 5.27) that only happens in the vicinity of the perigee (the effect gradually weak-
ens away from the perigee), unlike the case of the semi-major axis that, as explained previously along

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
ens away from the perigee), unlike the case of the semi-major axis that, as explained previously along
this section, affects the complete orbit (see Figure 5.20). This also explains why the decrease in per-
centages of compliance with the accuracy targets is more gentle than the dramatic degradation of the
maximum error in the computed observable along a complete orbit when 𝑒 is increased, as shown in
Figure 5.26.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

5.2. Sensitivity to the geometry of the simulated scenarios 69
Sensitivity to inclination angle ( 𝑖)
The conversion performances (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of com-
pliance with accuracy targets) of this model show a weak dependency on this parameter when the
eccentricity of the orbit is zero, as shown in Figure 5.28.
Figure 5.28: Performance figures of the corrective terms model. Sensitivity to ።. Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat with
፞ ዆ ኺ and ፑፀፀፍ ዆ ኾ኿ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T est cases: 677, 680 and 683.
However , whenever there is eccentricity ( 𝑒 ≠ 0 ), the conversion performances achieved by this
model worsen significantly. As commented before, this is due to the fact that the dynamics of the orbit
become much faster close to the perigee (and much slower close to the apogee) than in the nominal
circular orbit for which the numerical differentiation settings were optimised (according to the explana-
tion given in Section 3.2) and hence, the configured numerical differentiation settings are not capable
of properly capturing the dynamics of the orbital motion in that region. Under such circumstances,

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
of properly capturing the dynamics of the orbital motion in that region. Under such circumstances,
it is observed that (regardless of the RAAN) the model achieves better performances the higher the
inclination angle, as shown in Figure 5.29.
Figure 5.29: Performance figures of the corrective terms model. Sensitivity to ።. Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat with
፞ ዆ ኺ.ኾ and ፑፀፀፍ ዆ ኾ኿ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T est cases: 686, 689 and 692.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
70 5. Conversion results and discussion
This is explained because the effect of increasing the 𝑖 angle is reducing the magnitude of the velocity
of the orbiter with respect to the ground station along the line of sight (same as in the case of 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ -
see figure 5.11), which is equivalent to increasing a in the sense that it reduces the magnitude of the
range rate (Doppler observable) evolution in time. This leads to an improvement in the performance of
the numerical differentiation settings and consequently to an improvement of the error in the computed
observable.
Sensitivity to Right Ascension of the Ascending Node ( RAAN )
The conversion performances (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of com-
pliance with accuracy targets) of this model show a weak dependency on this parameter when the
eccentricity of the orbit is zero,as shown in Figure 5.30.
However , whenever the eccentricity of the orbit is bigger than zero, the conversion performances
achieved by this model degrade significantly, as shown in Figure 5.31. This is due to the fact that
the dynamics of the orbit become much faster close to the perigee (and much slower close to the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
the dynamics of the orbit become much faster close to the perigee (and much slower close to the
apogee) than in the nominal circular orbit for which the numerical differentiation settings were optimised
(according to the explanation given in Section 3.2) and hence, the configured numerical differentiation
settings are not capable of properly capturing the dynamics of the orbital motion in that region. Under
such circumstances, there is no general trend to explain the evolution of the maximum error in the
computed observable along a complete orbit as a function of the 𝑅𝐴𝐴𝑁 value. It depends on the
specific relative position of the ground station and the orbit of the simulated scenario.
As commented in the previous points, increasing the inclination angle produces the effect of reducing
the magnitude of the velocity of the orbiter with respect to the ground station along the line of sight
(same as in the case of 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ - see figure 5.11), which is equivalent to increasing 𝑎 in the sense that it
reduces the magnitude of the range rate (Doppler observable) evolution in time. The 𝑅𝐴𝐴𝑁 angle plays

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
reduces the magnitude of the range rate (Doppler observable) evolution in time. The 𝑅𝐴𝐴𝑁 angle plays
an analogous role in the orientation of the orbital plane with respect to the ground station, affecting
the magnitude of the input open-loop Doppler observations and consequently impacting on the level
of error in the computed observable. However , in the case of the RAAN, understanding what is its
influence on the performance of the model is not trivial and requires the study of the relative geometry
between the ground station and the orbit of every specific simulated scenario.
Figure 5.30: Performance figures of the corrective terms model. Sensitivity to ፑፀፀፍ . Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat with
፞ ዆ ኺ and ። ዆ ዃኺ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T est cases: 682 to 684.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package

metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
5.3. Comparison of the performance of both conversion models against each other 71
Figure 5.31: Performance figures of the corrective terms model. Sensitivity to ፑፀፀፍ . Realistic scenarios simulated in Tudat with
፞ ዆ ኺ.ኾ and ። ዆ ዃኺ ∘. ጂፓ ዆ ዀኺ፬ , ፭ᑠ ዆ ኻ፬ . T est cases: 691 to 693.
5.3. Comparison of the performance of both conversion models
against each other
This section is aimed at comparing the conversion performances achieved by the two studied data type
conversion mathematical models against each other based on the results obtained from the whole set of
executed tests. Moreover , some other considerations (different from the data conversion performances)
that would potentially affect the easiness of the practical implementation of both conversion models in
real life are presented in this section as well.
For the comparison of the performances of both models against each other , it is assumed that for
the integration-based model Simpson’s quadrature technique is always applied, since it clearly showed
to be able to achieve equal (in a few test cases) or better (in the majority of the executed test cases)

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
to be able to achieve equal (in a few test cases) or better (in the majority of the executed test cases)
performances along the whole set of tests than the trapezoidal quadrature technique, as discussed
along Sections 5.1 and 5.2.
It is worth recalling at this point that both models (the integration-based model only when applying
Simpson’s quadrature) are insensitive to the parameter 𝑡፨ (time step between input open-loop Doppler
observations) for the set of tested values (1, 2 and 5s).
The differences in performance between the two models concluded from the analysis of the results
of the executed tests are presented in section 5.3.1. Section 5.3.2 presents other considerations that
would potentially affect the easiness/simplicity/efficiency of the practical implementation of both data
type conversion models in real life.
5.3.1. Based on the results of the simulated scenarios
The main conclusions drawn from the comparative analysis of the conversion results achieved by each
of the two studied data type conversion models along the whole set of executed tests are the following.
• The integration-based model performs significantly better than the corrective terms model if the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
• The integration-based model performs significantly better than the corrective terms model if the
semi-major axis ( a) is set to lower values relative to the baseline value for which the numerical
differentiation settings were optimally tuned (keeping constant all the other configuration pa-
rameters). On the contrary, the conversion performances of both the integration-based and the
corrective terms models are very similar when 𝑎 is increased with respect to its baseline value.
This discussion can be consulted in Section 5.2.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
72 5. Conversion results and discussion
• The integration-based model performs significantly better (it is practically insensitive) than the
corrective terms model when there is eccentricity ( 𝑒 ≠ 0 ), if all the rest of the configuration
parameters are kept constant, as discussed in Section 5.2. The reason behind this conclusion is
the adequacy/inadequacy of the numerical differentiation settings, whose optimum configuration
is dependant on the dynamics of the orbital motion (that change all along the orbital period in
eccentric orbits), but all along this study they were kept constant according to the explanation
given in Section 3.2.
• In general, the integration-based model gives equal or better performances than the corrective
terms model, getting to the limit of highest measurable accuracy in the computed observable that
is constrained by the finite accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, as discussed all along Sections
5.1 and 5.2.
5.3.2. Based on other practical considerations
Apart from the convenience of one model or the other according to their achievable conversion per-
formances in the simulated scenarios, other practical considerations that should be taken into account

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
formances in the simulated scenarios, other practical considerations that should be taken into account
when determining the suitability of the studied data type conversion models for a real application are
the following:
Supporting the integration-based model
• This model is expected to be more robust than the corrective terms model against non-equispaced
distributions in time of the input open-loop Doppler data points because it is always possible
to build an interpolating parabola passing through any three consecutive data points, but it is
expected to be problematic to apply numerical differentiation to non-equispaced input data points
since it could happen that there is not available data at the times required by the implemented
stencil for the computation of the numerical derivatives of the range.
• The corrective terms model, to achieve its best performances, is constrained by the fact that it
needs to be given as input data at determined times for proper computation of the numerical
derivatives of the range. However , since the range data is obtained from a model of the orbital
motion, this issue is expected to be easily overcome by a proper integration of the algorithm of

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
motion, this issue is expected to be easily overcome by a proper integration of the algorithm of
the corrective terms model and the model of the orbital motion from which the range data is
obtained.
• For the practical implementation of the corrective terms model, the derivatives of the range are
not directly available from a given orbit solution and consequently they need to be computed
numerically. [ 7]
• For optimum performance, the corrective terms model requires the optimisation of the numerical
differentiation settings (stencil and time step for each derivatives order) for every specific relative
geometry between the orbit and the ground station thus requiring extra analysis effort.
• When applying the corrective terms model in real life [ 7], the observables that are used as input
to the orbit determination are obtained from the true trajectories, whereas the correction terms
proposed by the corrective terms model are computed from the current estimate of the dynamics
of both the transmitter and the receiver . This will introduce an inconsistency in the newly obtained
closed-loop Doppler observable, as the fundamental values used as input are now a mixture of

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
closed-loop Doppler observable, as the fundamental values used as input are now a mixture of
measures and modelled quantities. T o overcome this issue, the correction should be recomputed
after each iteration of the least-squares adjustment.
• Regarding the computational efficiency of both algorithms, my intuition, based on my knowledge
of the nature of both algorithms (presented in sections 3.1 and 4.1), is that the integration-based
model would be always significantly less computationally demanding than the corrective terms
model because it implies far less mathematical operations regardless of how the implementation
of the two models is done. However , none of the models has been submitted to any kind of
computational efficiency test because it is considered to be out of the scope of this study.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
5.3. Comparison of the performance of both conversion models against each other 73
• The corrective terms model has an extra parameter to be tuned: 𝑚 (highest order derivative
included in the summation - see Equation 4.1).
Supporting the corrective terms model
• T o be able to apply the integration-based model implementing Simpson’s quadrature technique,
the following condition must be satisfied:
Δ𝑇 ≥ 2 · 𝑡 ፨ (5.3)
It implies, as an example, that for the application of this data type conversion model, the in-
tegration time-span Δ𝑇 can not be set to the same value as 𝑡፨ (time separation between two
consecutive input open-loop Doppler data points), as it could be done if applying the corrective
terms conversion model.
For the application of the integration-based model implementing Simpson’s quadrature the inte-
gration time-span Δ𝑇 has necessarily to be set to a value equal or bigger than two times 𝑡፨.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
6
Conclusions and recommendations
6.1. Conclusions
The main conclusion drawn from the study presented in this report, that answers the main research
question of this work, is that the two examined data type conversion mathematical models are capable
of achieving data type conversion performances with accuracy of the order of magnitude of the pur-
sued accuracy targets under certain circumstances concerning the planetary geometry of the scenario
and the configuration of the inherent parameters of the models. Two factors have been identified to
be of crucial importance for achieving high data type conversion performances: for the integration-
based model, the quadrature technique applied; and for the corrective terms model, the numerical
differentiation settings (stencil and time step for each derivative order).
However , according to the research questions presented in Section 1.3, the goals of this study were
multiple. For this reason, it is considered convenient to conclude individually about each of them.
How can equivalent closed-loop Doppler data be obtained, by means of a mathematical model,
from an open-loop Doppler data set?

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
How can equivalent closed-loop Doppler data be obtained, by means of a mathematical model,
from an open-loop Doppler data set?
In this study, two different approaches [ 7] have been introduced, implemented, validated and tested
against a whole set of scenarios with different configuration settings and input orbital geometries.
The integration-based model converts input open-loop Doppler observations into equivalent closed-
loop observations by numerically integrating the input observable in time and dividing the result by the
time-span of the integration window. Two different numerical integration methods have been tested
for this conversion model: trapezoidal and Simpson’s (both introduced in Section 3.1.4). The latter
has shown to achieve, in general, significantly better conversion performances than the former , and it
has also shown to be much more robust to variations in the geometry of the simulated scenarios, as
discussed in Chapter 5.
The corrective terms model performs the data type conversion by adding a finite amount of terms
to the input open-loop data, coming from the truncation of a T aylor series expansion of the range. The

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
to the input open-loop data, coming from the truncation of a T aylor series expansion of the range. The
major issue in the application of this approach is the computation of the numerical derivatives of the
range that appear in the terms of the T aylor series because they are not directly available from a given
orbit solution. As discussed in Chapter 5, the performance of this model is very sensitive to variations
in some geometric features of the simulated scenarios.
What are the performances of such mathematical models?
The performance of each of the models has been assessed following the performance analysis strategy
presented in Section 3.2. It consists of two sensitivity analysis performed for each of the two models
(4 in total), each of the two different sensitivity analysis focusing on a different group of parameters:
inherent configuration parameters of the models or geometric features of the simulated scenarios.
In addition, each of the two different sensitivity analysis has been performed by including data sets
generated by two different processes: simulating analytically simplistic scenarios, or simulating more
realistic scenarios in Tudat.
75

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
76 6. Conclusions and recommendations
The computed closed-loop observable ( ℎ፜) has been compared against the classical model for pro-
cessing closed-loop data ( ℎ፜,፫) proposed by Moyer ([ 18]) and presented in Section 2.2.1 of this report,
and some figures of merit (explained in Section 3.2.4) have computed based on the difference of both
data types, aiming at a consistent comparison of the conversion performances of the models in differ-
ent test cases and also at a consistent comparison of the conversion performances achieved by each
of the models against each other for analogous tests.
By testing the two data type conversion models against the whole set of test cases with different
parameter settings (geometric and of the models), the following conclusions have been made about the
performances achievable by each of the models as a function of the parameters varied in the sensitivity
analysis.
• In all the tests to which the integration-based model implementing Simpsons’ quadrature has
been submitted, it has achieved a maximum conversion error (point by point difference ℎ፜,፫ − ℎ፜)
along a complete orbit lower than 20% (accuracy target 2 as defined in Section 3.2.4) of the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
along a complete orbit lower than 20% (accuracy target 2 as defined in Section 3.2.4) of the
expected accuracy of the instrumentation ( 10ዅ኿ 𝑚/𝑠 for Δ𝑇 = 60𝑠 ). Moreover , in the test cases
with Δ𝐷 lower than its baseline value, the maximum conversion error along a complete orbit was
even below the 5% (accuracy target 1) of the expected accuracy of the instrumentation. This is
due to the limit in the measurable accuracy of the computed observable due to the finite accuracy
in the computation of ℎ፜,፫.
In addition, in all the tests to which this model has been submitted it has achieved percentages
of compliance with the target accuracy 1 of 70% or more, and 100% for the accuracy targets 2
and 3 (±5%, ±20% and ±100% of the expected accuracy of the instrumentation, respectively).
• In the tests to which the corrective terms model has been submitted, it has been observed
that if the semi-major axis ( 𝑎) is not reduced with respect to the baseline value with which the
numerical differentiation settings were optimised and the eccentricity ( 𝑒) is set to zero, the data
type conversion performances achieved by the model are very similar to those achieved by the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
type conversion performances achieved by the model are very similar to those achieved by the
integration-based model: maximum conversion error along a complete orbit lower than 20% of
the expected accuracy of the instrumentation. In the test cases with Δ𝐷 lower than its baseline
value, this figure of merit goes even below 5% of the expected accuracy of the instrumentation.
The reason for it is also the limit in the measurable accuracy of the computed observable due to
the finite accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫.
Regarding the percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets, this model achieves very
slightly worse performances than the other for the above mentioned subset of test cases: 50%
or more for the target 1, and 100% (or very close to it) for the accuracy targets 2 and 3 (±5%,
±20% and ±100% of the expected accuracy of the instrumentation, respectively).
In the rest of test cases (those with 𝑎 lower than its baseline value or 𝑒 bigger than zero) the
performances (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with
the accuracy targets) are strongly degraded as discussed in Section 5.2.2.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
the accuracy targets) are strongly degraded as discussed in Section 5.2.2.
How do the parameters of the models influence their performances?
This specific issue is addressed in Section 5.1: Sensitivity of the performance of the models to their
inherent configuration parameters .
According to the performance analysis strategy presented in Section 3.2, not all the parameters
involved in the problem have been varied in the sensitivity analysis for practical reasons. In particular ,
for the inherent configuration parameters of the models, those that were common to the two models
(𝑡፨ and Δ𝑇) have been included in the sensitive analysis for comparison purposes. In addition, as
commented before, for the numerical integrals of the integration-based model, two different methods
have been applied: trapezoidal and Simpson’s.
Although it is discussed in more detail in Section 5.1, the highlights of the influence of each of these
parameters in the performance of the models is briefly summarised here below for completeness.
• 𝑡፨: When applying Simpson’s quadrature, the performances achieved by the integration-based

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
• 𝑡፨: When applying Simpson’s quadrature, the performances achieved by the integration-based
model (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with the target
levels of accuracy) are insensitive to variations in the parameter for the range of tested values,

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
6.1. Conclusions 77
whereas the performances achieved by the same model when applying trapezoidal quadrature
worsen significantly as 𝑡፨ is increased.
The corrective terms model has shown to be completely insensitive to variations in 𝑡፨ for the
range of tested values.
• Δ𝑇: In general it is concluded that the maximum error of the integration-based model along
a complete orbit improves (decreases) as Δ𝑇 increases, regardless of the numerical quadrature
technique applied. The reduction is by the same factor by which Δ𝑇 is increased. Regarding the
percentages of compliance with the target levels of accuracy, on the one hand it is concluded
that, when applying Simpson’s integration they improve as ΔT is increased. This coincides with
the expectation since the errors obtained with Simpson’s technique (random noise) decrease by a
factor of ኻ
ጂፓ (the errors are driven by the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫, as explained
in Section 4.2) as ΔT increases, whereas the target accuracy levels decrease by a factor of ኻ
√ጂፓ as
ΔT increases, meaning that the decrease in maximum error of the observable along a complete

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
√ጂፓ as
ΔT increases, meaning that the decrease in maximum error of the observable along a complete
orbit is faster or stronger than the reduction of the target accuracy levels as a function of ΔT
(given by Eq. 3.23). On the other hand, when applying trapezoidal integration, there is not
a clear trend in the evolution of the percentages of compliance as a function of ΔT , being the
observed behaviour also a function of 𝑡፨.
Regarding the corrective terms model , in most of the cases, it has been observed that the maxi-
mum error of along an orbit improves (decreases) as Δ𝑇 increases regardless of 𝑡፨. The reduction
is by the same factor by which Δ𝑇 is increased, and the reason is, again, the limit in the mea-
surable accuracy of the computed observable due to the finite accuracy in the computation of
ℎ፜,፫. There are, however , two clear exceptions to this behaviour: when the semi-major axis is
decreased or the eccentricity is increased with respect to the baseline values ( 𝑎 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚
and 𝑒 = 0 ) for which the numerical settings where optimised. In those cases the conversion
performances of the model are strongly degraded. The maximum error along a complete orbit

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
performances of the model are strongly degraded. The maximum error along a complete orbit
increased by an order of magnitude when 𝑎 was halved with respect to its baseline value. When
the eccentricity was set to 0.4, the maximum error increased also by 1 to 2 orders of magnitude,
and more dramatically for 𝑒 = 0.8 the maximum error increased up to the order of units. In
addition, an evident difference between the two discussed exceptions has been observed: the
decrease of the percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets as Δ𝑇 increases is stronger
in the former exception than in the latter , being it due to the fact that, while the bad performance
of the model due to the increase in 𝑒 is localised around the perigee, the decrease in performance
due to a reduction of 𝑎 affects the whole orbit.
• Numerical quadrature method : This applies only to the integration-based model. The main con-
clusions with regard to this configuration parameter is that the quadrature scheme has a crucial
impact on the performance levels achieved by the model; and that Simpson’s numerical quadra-
ture achieves significantly better performances and has a much more robust behaviour than

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
ture achieves significantly better performances and has a much more robust behaviour than
trapezoidal quadrature along the whole set of executed tests.
Do the characteristics of the input data set influence the performances of the models? If so,
how?
This specific issue is addressed in Section 5.2: Sensitivity of the performance of the models to the
geometry of the simulated scenarios .
Again, according to the performance analysis strategy presented in Section 3.2, not all the param-
eters involved in the problem have been varied in the sensitivity analysis for practical reasons. In
particular , regarding the geometric features of the simulated scenarios, the same set of parameters
have been varied for testing the two data type conversion algorithms: semi-major axis ( 𝑎), inclination
(𝑖), right ascension of the ascending node ( 𝑅𝐴𝐴𝑁), eccentricity (𝑒), interplanetary distance between the
ground station and the central body of the orbiter ( Δ𝐷) and orbital plane inclination angle in simplistic
analytic scenarios ( 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ).
Although it is discussed in more detail in Section 5.2, the influence of the parameters that have a

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
analytic scenarios ( 𝑖ፚ፧ፚ፥ ).
Although it is discussed in more detail in Section 5.2, the influence of the parameters that have a
stronger impact on the performance of the models is briefly summarised here below for completeness.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
78 6. Conclusions and recommendations
• Semi-major axis ( 𝑎): The measured performance of the integration-based model is insensitive
to variations in 𝑎 when applying Simpson’s quadrature technique. When applying trapezoidal
quadrature, the higher the value of a relative to its baseline value ( 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚) the
better the performance of the model (both in terms of maximum error in a complete orbit and
percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets). This dependency is very strong.
On the other hand, the performances of the corrective terms model are, in general, high (maxi-
mum error along a complete orbit smaller than the most demanding accuracy target and around
100% compliance with the accuracy targets along the whole orbit) for 𝑎 settings equal or bigger
than its baseline value ( 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚). On the contrary, its performances degrade signifi-
cantly as 𝑎 is reduced with respect to its baseline value 𝐴. The maximum error along a complete
orbit increased by an order of magnitude when 𝑎 was halved with respect to its baseline value.
It is interesting to note that this effect affects the whole orbit, unlike the case of the eccentricity,

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
It is interesting to note that this effect affects the whole orbit, unlike the case of the eccentricity,
whose negative impact on the performance of this model is local.
• Eccentricity ( 𝑒): The performances of the integration-based model (both maximum error along a
complete orbit and percentages of compliance with the target levels of accuracy) are insensitive
(it showed negligible changes) to variations in eccentricity when applying Simpson’s quadrature.
On the contrary, when applying trapezoidal quadrature, the maximum error in a complete orbit
increases dramatically and, contrary to it, it has been observed that the percentages of compliance
with the accuracy targets improve as 𝑒 increases.
Regarding the corrective terms model , there is a clear , general trend for the performance of the
model as a function of 𝑒: the higher the eccentricity of the orbit, the worse the performances
of the model (both maximum error along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with
the accuracy targets). This dependency is very strong. It has been observed that when the
eccentricity was set to 0.4, the maximum error increased by 1 to 2 orders of magnitude with

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
eccentricity was set to 0.4, the maximum error increased by 1 to 2 orders of magnitude with
respect to the reference case ( 𝑒 = 0 ), and more dramatically for 𝑒 = 0.8 the maximum error
increased up to the order of units. It is interesting to note that the degradation in the performance
of this model due to the eccentricity is a local phenomenon that only happens in the vicinity of the
perigee (the effect gradually weakens away from the perigee), unlike the case of the semi-major
axis, whose negative effect takes place along the whole orbit.
• Interplanetary distance ( Δ𝐷): In general, for the integration-based model , it is observed that
reducing Δ𝐷 with respect to its baseline value ( 𝐷 = 2.25 · 10 ኻኻ𝑚) improves the measured perfor-
mances of the model regardless of the quadrature method applied. The reduction is by the same
factor by which Δ𝐷 is decreased. This due to the limited accuracy in the computation of ℎ፜,፫,
accuracy that increases as the interplanetary distance is reduced, according to the explanation
given in Section 4.2.
Regarding the corrective terms model , the same trend has been observed (the reason being the

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
given in Section 4.2.
Regarding the corrective terms model , the same trend has been observed (the reason being the
same as for the integration-based conversion model) except for the cases in which the semi-major
axis was reduced with respect to the baseline value ( 𝐴 = 9.2476 · 10 ዀ𝑚) for which the numerical
differentiation settings were optimised.
Is there any systematic approach to optimise the performances of the models?
A completely satisfactory answer to this question has not been reached after the development of this
study. However , valuable insight on how an important part of the parameters that play a role in the
problem impact the performance of the models has been gained with the sensitivity analysis presented
in this report. In particular , the conclusion that the time step for the numerical derivatives of the
range in elliptical orbits must be configured differently for perigee and apogee (or gradually adapted
all along the orbital period) is considered to be very valuable. The analysis of how the orbital velocity
(or , equivalently, the semi-major axis) impacts the quality of the numerical derivatives of the range has
been very enriching as well.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
(or , equivalently, the semi-major axis) impacts the quality of the numerical derivatives of the range has
been very enriching as well.
All in all, it is considered that this work can be a solid foundation based on which further analysis
can be carried out aiming at finding or developing a systematic approach to optimise the performances
of these data type conversion models for any scenario.

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
6.2. Recommendations for future work 79
Other concluding remarks
Apart from the answer to the research questions given above, some other important conclusions that
have been made based on the work performed along this study are:
• For the integration-based model , it has been observed that the performances (both maximum
error along a complete orbit and percentages of compliance with the accuracy targets) achieved
by applying Simpson’s quadrature are in all the tests equal or better than those obtained by
applying trapezoidal quadrature.
• Regarding the comparison of the performances of each of the models against each other , the
integration-based model with Simpson’s quadrature has shown be the most robust one against
variation of any of the parameters included in the sensitivity analysis.
• Apart from the conclusions drawn from the results of sensitivity analysis of the simulated sce-
narios, there are other practical considerations that would impact the decision of which model to
implement in real life (discussed in Section 5.3.2), most of which are favourable to the integration-
based model (with Simpson’s quadrature).
6.2. Recommendations for future work

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
based model (with Simpson’s quadrature).
6.2. Recommendations for future work
From the results reported in this thesis, a number of open questions whose answer would be an
interesting complement to this study arise:
• It would have been interesting to perform this whole study without the limit in the accuracy (or
at least with less limitation) for the computation of ℎ፜,፫. This would potentially allow finding the
maximum capabilities of the models, that in many cases kept unknown because when compar-
ing the computed observable ( ℎ፜) against the classic model for processing closed-loop Doppler
data ( ℎ፜,፫) the error in the computation of the latter overrode the error in the calculation of the
computed observable.
• It would be very interesting to improve the implementation of the corrective terms model by
developing an algorithm that automatically optimises the 𝛿𝑡፧ for each derivative order as a func-
tion of the local orbital velocity, instantaneous line-of-sight velocity or other related parameter
that could help reducing the error when the dynamics of the orbit are changed significantly with

metadata.creator: LaTeX with hyperref package
metadata.author: M. Mezo Gómez
metadata.title: Open- and closed-loop Doppler data modelling for spacecraft tracking

text:
that could help reducing the error when the dynamics of the orbit are changed significantly with
respect to the case for which the numerical differentiation settings were optimised (for example
between perigee and apogee for eccentric orbits, or when varying the semi-major axis).
• Other interesting extension of this study would include testing the performances of these same
models but with other numerical techniques for differentiation and integration.
• It would also be of interest to perform an analysis of the computational efficiency of every data
type conversion model.
• Finally, it would be of great value to test the models against a real input data set.

A
Test cases settings
In this Annex, several tables containing the configuration settings of every test case executed is this
study are included. Column T estindicates a code that identifies every individual test. Column Model
specifies the data type conversion model that was submitted to that test: number 1 refers to the
integration-based model while number 2 refers to the corrective terms model. Column Sens. to in-
dicates whether the test is part of the sensitivity analysis to the inherent parameters of the model
(Model) or it is part of the sensitivity analysis to the geometry of the simulated scenarios (Geometry).
Column Type indicates whether the input data set was generated analytically of from a simulation ex-
ecuted in Tudat. Column Subcase contains a number identifying the configured geometric settings in
the tests corresponding to the sensitivity analysis to inherent configuration parameters of the models
or , analogously, identifying the configuration of the inherent parameters of the model for the tests
corresponding to the sensitivity analysis to geometric features of the simulated scenarios. The content

text:
Escuela de Ingenierías I.I. 
 
 
 
 
Industrial, Informática y Aeroespacial 
 
 
  
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
GRADO EN INGENIERÍA AEROESPACIAL 
 
 
 
Trabajo de Fin de Grado 
 
 
 
ESTUDIO DE VIABILIDAD TÉCNICA PARA LA 
INSTALACIÓN DE UN BANCO DE ENSAYOS PARA 
PEQUEÑOS TURBORREACTORES  
EN LA UNIVERSIDAD DE LEÓN 
 
 
 
Autor: D. Manuel Mezo Gómez  
 
Tutor: D. Juan Carlos Celada Fernández

Página 1 
 
 
 
UNIVERSIDAD DE LEÓN 
Escuela de Ingenierías I.I. 
GRADO EN INGENIERÍA AEROESPACIAL 
Trabajo de Fin de Grado  
 
ALUMNO: D. Manuel Mezo Gómez  
TUTOR: D. Juan Carlos Celada Fernández 
TÍTULO: Estudio de viabilidad técnica para la ins talación de un banco de 
ensayos para pequeños turborreactores en la Universidad de León 
CONVOCATORIA: Julio, 2015 
RESUMEN: 
El objetivo del estudio es determinar la viabilidad de la instalación de un banco 
de ensayos para pequeños turborreactores en la Un iversidad de León, desde un 
punto de vista técnico. Por otra parte, se pretende que el trabajo constituya 
una base sólida de conocimientos relacionados con la materia. El estudio 
contempla los problemas asociados a la operación de un turborreactor en un 
recinto cerrado y las condiciones que deben satisfacerse para lograr su correcto 
funcionamiento. Además, presenta algunas soluciones para mitigar los efectos 
adversos derivados del funcionamiento de este tipo de motores en instalaciones 
interiores.

Introducción 
El objetivo de este trabajo es determinar la viabilidad de la  instalación de un 
banco de ensayos para pequeños turborreactores en la Universidad de León , 
desde un punto de vista técnico.  
El estudio parte de la ubicación y los planos del edificio selecciona do para la 
instalación y continúa con la realización de un estudio de mercado para la 
selección de los subsistemas (motor y banco) más adecuados.  
Contempla la problemática asociada al funcionamiento de este tipo de motores  
en instalaciones cerradas , la no rmativa relativa a su operación y las soluciones 
existentes para mitigar o eliminar los efectos adversos  derivados de su 
funcionamiento en un recinto cerrado. 
Finalmente se concluye que la instalación del banco de ensayos  es viable desde 
un punto de vista técnico.
